Admitting Diagnosis:
1. Intertrochanteric fracture left hip
Discharge Diagnoses:
Active Hospital Problems
 *Closed left hip fracture, initial encounter (HCC)
Hospital Course: 49 y.o. female with a past history significant for iron-deficiency anemia/gastric sleeve
surgery, migraine admitted on 2/25/2023 with pain left hip after tripping and falling over speeding bump of the
parking lot At Wallgreen , immediately she felt pain on the left hip with unable to move or walk brought to the
emergency room via her daughter car,
Emergency room course
Vitals T 98.4°, HR 118, RR 11, BP 147/67
Lab sodium 135 hemoglobin 7.2 hematocrit 23.8
X-ray left hip showed intertrochanteric fracture of the left femoral neck
CT pelvis shows
Acute comminuted intratrochanteric fracture of the left femur.
Treatment given morphine
On also ketamine muscle relaxant
On February 26, 2023
Underwent open reduction and internal fixation of intertrochanteric fracture.

Discharge Summary by Ibrahim, Kamal, MD at 02/28/23 0913 (continued)
VIEWS: Two views left hip. COMPARISON: No comparison. HISTORY: Postop left hip.
FINDINGS/IMPRESSION: Hardware present within the left hip related to open reduction and internal fixation of
intertrochanteric fracture. Hardware is intact and in expected position. Essure device projected over the
pelvis. Visualized bony pelvic rings are intact.
CT PELVIS WO CONTRAST
Result Date: 2/25/2023
INDICATION: FX, needs further eval COMPARISON: Radiographs same day TECHNIQUE: Volumetric CT
scan of the abdomen, and pelvis without intravenous contrast. 2D axial, sagittal, and coronal reformats were
performed. FINDINGS: Bladder: Unremarkable. Solid pelvic organs: Unremarkable. Visualized Bowel: No
dilation or wall thickening. No extraluminal fluid or gas. Lymph nodes: None enlarged by CT size criteria.
Vessels: Unremarkable. Bones: There is an acute comminuted intratrochanteric fracture of the left femur.
Adjacent small hematoma and soft tissue swelling noted.
IMPRESSION: Acute comminuted intratrochanteric fracture of the left femur.
Risk of readmission:

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Chief Complaint
Patient presents with
Fall
Hip Pain
HPI
Kimberly K Brasher is a 49 y.o. female who presents for evaluation of left hip pain. She was leaving work,
tripped on curb and fell. Denies head injury. No headache or neck pain. States has pain exclusively to L hip
and denies other injuries. No anticoagulant use.

Extremities/Musculoskeletal: Intact distal pulses, No cyanosis, No clubbing. Very painful L hip, held in

Neurologic: Alert & oriented x 3, Normal motor function, Normal sensory function, No focal deficits noted.
Psychiatric: Affect normal, Judgment normal, Mood normal.
Ketamine was given for pain control while we repositioned the LLE for comfort. She was having severe
cramping in the L thigh and hip and buttock due to the fracture and the positioning of the leg which she would
not allow us to manipulate prior to sedation. Adequate sedation achieved, Leg repositioned and patient much
more comfortable.

Medical Decision Making
Ms. Brasher presented after falling today. She reported a mechanical fall, no syncope. She had left hip pain,
we did discover a left hip fracture. I discussed with orthopedics provider on-call and she will be admitted, plan
for surgery tomorrow at 8:00 a.m..
Closed fracture of left hip, initial encounter (HCC): acute illness or injury
Amount and/or Complexity of Data Reviewed
External Data Reviewed: labs.
Labs: ordered.
 Details: CBC demonstrated anemia, hemoglobin was 7. Patient reported history of anemia that required
blood transfusions due to iron deficiency in the past. She felt that this was closed her baseline. I examined her
again myself after this result and did not note any signs of internal bleeding. She was also examined by an
orthopedist in internist and we will do suspect that this is simply iron deficiency. I ordered 1 unit of blood prior
to surgery.
Radiology: ordered.
 Details: Ct was ordered to further evaluate the fracture demonstrated on Xray.
Risk
Prescription drug management.

HPI
HPI
Kimberly K Brasher is a 49 y.o. female with PMH notable for anemia and hx of gastric bypass who presented to
Deaconess Henderson today with left hip pain after tripping and falling over speed bump in parking lot.
Daugter brought to ER where workup confirmed left intertrochanteric hip fracture. Hx of transfusion in past due
to anemia. Daughter states no transfusions for some time now. We have been asked to evaluate the patient's
orthopaedic injury while being treated by hospitalist service.

Mechanical fall

Plan
Risks and benefits were explained to these included but not limited to bleeding, infection, damage to the
surrounding structures, need for further procedures, anesthetic effect or events, reflex sympathetic dystrophy,
DVT, PE, pneumonia, pain, decreased range of motion, decreased functional strength, functional impairment,
incapacitation, disability, malunion, nonunion, hardware failure, hardware breakage, hardware migration,
fracture, re-fracture, loosening of hardware, need for revision surgeries, dissatisfaction, unforeseen problems in
the future, unforeseen complications in the future, arthrosis, arthritis, fibrosis, stiffness, nerve damage, blood
vessel damage, hematoma, seroma, wound problems, wound dehiscence, numbness, tingling, capsular,
ligamentous, cartilage, bony tendinous, muscular damage, limb inequality, cosmetic deformities. At no time
were guarantees stated or implied.
Plan for operative fixation of fracture tomorrow morning with Dr. Lowery
NPO at MN
Consent orders placed
OR staff notified
Plan discussed with :patient and family
Diagnosis and treatment explained in detail and questions answered.

SURGEON:
Douglas Lowery, MD
HPI:
A 49-year-old female who sustained a closed left intertrochanteric hip
fracture. Risks, benefits and alternatives explained to the patient. Marked
preoperatively for IM nailing of hip fracture. See the H and P for details.
PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:
Left closed intertrochanteric hip fracture.
POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:
Left closed intertrochanteric hip fracture.
PROCEDURE:
Left intramedullary nailing of hip fracture laterally.
None.
BLOOD LOSS:
Minimal.
SPECIMENS:
None.
FINDINGS:
Closed intertrochanteric hip fracture.
COMPONENTS USED:
Synthes short trochanteric femoral nail, 9 mm x 135 degrees, with an 85 mm
head and neck device, and a 34 mm distal locking screw.
MEDICATIONS:
Preoperative antibiotics.
DESCRIPTION OF PROCEDURE:
The patient was brought to the operative room, prepped and draped in normal
sterile routine orthopedic fashion following anesthesia per the anesthesia
department. The patient was placed on the fracture table, and appropriate
Tue Feb 20, 2024 1:28 PM

DEACONESS HENDERSON

1305 N ELM ST
HENDERSON KY 42420-
2783
IP Encounter Report
Brasher, Kimberly K
MRN: 469590, DOB: 3/25/1973, Sex: F
Adm: 2/25/2023, D/C: 2/28/2023
Op Note - Operative and Procedure Notes (continued)
Op Note signed by Lowery, Douglas, MD at 03/03/23 0836 (continued)
reduction maneuvers were performed to align the hip fracture. A small
incision was made, based on the greater trochanter just proximal and
posterior. A sharp dissection was taken down to the gluteal fascia, which was
 then split in line with its fibers. The greater trochanter was identified
with direct palpation. FluoroScan was utilized to facilitate placement of a
guidewire in the greater trochanter at the appropriate place and taken from
the greater trochanter into the femoral shaft. This was checked on AP and
lateral radiographs and found to be appropriate. Next, the opening reamer was
 used to open up the insertion site and to make sure there was an appropriate
amount of room for the nail to enter the shaft. After appropriate
preparation, the nail listed in findings section was placed in the canal with
attention to depth of insertion. This was checked on AP and lateral
radiographs. Through a second small stab incision on the lateral aspect of
the flare of the greater trochanter, we bring our guidewire from the flare of
the greater trochanter up through the femoral neck and into the femoral head
on AP and lateral radiographs. Once an appropriate measurement was obtained,
drill and insertion of the head and neck component was placed. Once this was
appropriate and in place, we tightened the locking screw. Once both of these
were checked on AP and lateral radiographs and found to be in appropriate
position, we then used the guide on static and, with a small stab incision,
made our interlocking screw distally. We checked this on AP and lateral
radiographs as well. Copious irrigation with normal saline then followed. We
 removed the insertion hardware. We made sure that our last set of AP and
lateral radiographs showed good hardware placement. We then copiously
irrigated with normal saline once again, closed the fascia with 2-0 Vicryl in
interrupted deep buried fashion and closed the skin. The dressings included
coverlets, and the patient was then taken to the recovery room.
______________________________
Douglas Lowery, MD

Physical Exam
Inspection: Skin warm to touch. Normal color and turgor. Skin is intact/incision appears to be healing.
Wound: Dressings in place, dry with minimal drainage. No signs of infection.
Palpation: No calf tenderness or signs of DVT.
ROM: Displays AROM dorsiflexion upper and lower extremities bilaterally.
Neurovascular: Distal light sensation is intact. Distal pulses intact. No signs of compartment syndrome noted.
ASSESSMENT: Status Post: Left hip intramedullary nail
PLAN:
Labs, imaging, and medications reviewed today. Hemoglobin 7.9 this morning. Appears much less sedated
today compared to yesterday. States that she is ready to leave hospital. Is declining facility level placement
for rehab needs. States she plans to go to outpatient therapy and family member will be taking care of her
upon discharge home. We had briefly discussed yesterday that due to her chronic anemia we may discharge
her home on full strength aspirin but since her hemoglobin is improving I feel that it is better to place her on
Xarelto for the next 30 days for DVT prophylaxis. Explained to patient and family members this will provide her
with better protection against blood clots. They were both in agreement. PT/OT therapy order placed and
printed for patient to take to preferred outpatient therapy facility. Electronic Rx of Xarelto sent to pharmacy on
file. Patient to follow up in our office in 2 weeks for recheck with x-rays and staple removal. To call the office
with any concerns. Being discharged home today. Discussed treatment plan with patient/family. Answered all
questions to the satisfaction of patient/family. Nursing/rehab staff to contact with any questions and/or
concerns.
Weight Bearing Status: Weight-bearing as tolerated left lower extremity.
ROM: AROM as tolerated to left lower extremity.
Splint/Brace: Rolling walker
DVT Prophylaxis: Lovenox to transition to Xarelto on discharge

CBC drawn this morning just resulted. Hemoglobin 7.8, this is up from 7.2 on 02/25 but patient did receive a
blood transfusion prior to surgery. Would recommend repeating labs in morning to evaluate if another
transfusion may be needed. PT visit this morning was essentially postponed due to patient not tolerating any
movement due to pain.
Encouraged patient to use oral pain medications ordered instead of IV Dilaudid as this seems to be causing
more sedation than as needed. Would like to continue the subcutaneous Lovenox for DVT prophylaxis while in
hospital. We may switch to full-strength aspirin twice daily on discharge for continued prophylaxis as there is a
chronic anemia concern.
Will continue to follow.

Patient: Kimberly K Brasher
Age: 49 y.o.
DOB: 3/25/1973
Narrative
Pt was admitted with left hip fx following a fall. She received ORIF. Pt requires encouragement for any activity
with nurse present to provide pain medication. She voices want to try things on her own and does not tolerate
touching of left LE. After extensive time with attempt EOB, pt refuses further assistance with transfer, even with
reports of unable to continue. Finally asking to be left alone. Pt left in bed with friend present and PCT plan to
check back with her. Will continue to follow.
Recommendations
Therapy Recommendations: Pt would benefit from continued therapy at a facility level
Outcome Measure
Basic Mobility Raw Score: 6 (02/27/23 0900)
Basic Mobility T-Scale Score: 16.59 (02/27/23 0900)
Plan
Treatment/Interventions: Transfer training, Balance training, LE strengthening/ROM, Endurance training,
Patient/Family education, Gait training, ROM, Bed mobility, Neuro re-education, Home exercise program, Body
mechanics, Safety education
PT Frequency: 5x/week, 7x/week
Prior Level of Function
Permanent Residence: Home
Home Layout: One level, Stairs to enter with rails
Home Equipment Available : Rolling Walker, Shower chair

Author: Goebel, Aubrey, OT Service: Author Type: Occupational Therapist
Filed: 02/27/23 0939 Date of Service: 02/27/23 0919 Status: Addendum
Editor: Goebel, Aubrey, OT (Occupational Therapist)
Related Notes: Original Note by Goebel, Aubrey, OT (Occupational Therapist) filed at 02/27/23 0938
OCCUPATIONAL THERAPY
ASSESSMENT NOTE
Narrative
Pt admitted after fall resulting in L hip fx. She underwent ORIF on L hip 2/26/23. She is WBAT to LLE,
RN is providing IV pain meds upon arrival. Pt requires increased time to participate with therapy due to wanting
to don underwear and pants and being very particular about mobility and using her L leg. She insists on doing
as much as possible for herself before requiring assist from PT/OT. She is able to scoot self to side of bed
supervision/touch assist with increased time. Attempted to assist pt with sitting EOB with increased time and
assist x3 people. Pt becomes very anxious, pushing back, and screaming in pain. Did not attempt further
mobility due to safety. Will attempt further mobility at a later time as able.
Recommendations
Recommendation: Pt would benefit from continued therapy at a facility level
Outcome Measure
Daily Activity Raw Score : 15
Daily Activity T-Scale Score: 36.69
Assessment
Assessment: Decreased ADL status, Decreased UE strength, Decreased safe judgement during ADL,
Decreased activity tolerance, Decreased self-care trans, Decreased high-level ADLs
Plan
OT Frequency: 3x/week, 5x/week
Treatment Interventions: ADL retraining, Functional transfer training, UE strengthening/ROM, Activity
tolerance, Patient/Family training, Equipment eval/education, Fine motor coordination activities, Compensatory
technique education, Energy conservations/work simplification, HEP, IADLs, Safety education
Prior Level of Function
Self Care (New): Independent
Indoor Mobility (Ambulation) (New): Independent
Stairs (New): Independent
Functional Cognition (New): Independent

Result Date: 2/26/2023
VIEWS: Two views left hip. COMPARISON: No comparison. HISTORY: Postop left hip.
FINDINGS/IMPRESSION: Hardware present within the left hip related to open reduction and internal fixation of
intertrochanteric fracture. Hardware is intact and in expected position. Essure device projected over the
pelvis. Visualized bony pelvic rings are intact.
CT PELVIS WO CONTRAST
Result Date: 2/25/2023
INDICATION: FX, needs further eval COMPARISON: Radiographs same day TECHNIQUE: Volumetric CT
scan of the abdomen, and pelvis without intravenous contrast. 2D axial, sagittal, and coronal reformats were
performed. FINDINGS: Bladder: Unremarkable. Solid pelvic organs: Unremarkable. Visualized Bowel: No
dilation or wall thickening. No extraluminal fluid or gas. Lymph nodes: None enlarged by CT size criteria.
Vessels: Unremarkable. Bones: There is an acute comminuted intratrochanteric fracture of the left femur.
Adjacent small hematoma and soft tissue swelling noted.
IMPRESSION: Acute comminuted intratrochanteric fracture of the left femur.

NARRATIVE:
49 year old female who presented with a Fx Hip.
ASSESSMENT:
Met with patient's friend at bedside. She reports the plan moving forward is for the patient to go back home with
her in Bowling Green , Ky where she will see to patient's care and get her in outpatient PT. Her friend states
she has a walker , BSC , and raised toilet seat for use at her home for the patient as she recently had a hip
replacement in the past. Spoke with B. Gish APRN/ Ortho and he is aware of this plan and agreeable. Will
follow.

Home with friend

Volumetric CT scan of the abdomen, and pelvis without intravenous contrast. 2D
axial, sagittal, and coronal reformats were performed.
FINDINGS:
Bladder: Unremarkable.
Solid pelvic organs: Unremarkable.
Visualized Bowel: No dilation or wall thickening. No extraluminal fluid or gas.
Lymph nodes: None enlarged by CT size criteria.
Vessels: Unremarkable.
Bones: There is an acute comminuted intratrochanteric fracture of the left
femur. Adjacent small hematoma and soft tissue swelling noted.

Acute comminuted intratrochanteric fracture of the left femur.

Attached Information Methocarbamol (By mouth) (English)
Methocarbamol (By mouth)
Methocarbamol (meth-oh-KAR-ba-mol)
Treats muscle pain and spasms.
Brand Name(s): Robaxin, Robaxin-750
There may beother brand names for this medicine.
When This Medicine Should Not Be Used:
This medicine is not right for everyone. Do not use it if you had an allergic reaction to methocarbamol.
How to Use This Medicine:
Tablet
* Take your medicine as directed. Your dose may need to bechanged several times to find what works best for you.
* Missed dose: Take a dose as soon as you remember. If it is almost time for your next dose, wait until then and
take a regular dose. Do not take extra medicine to make up for a missed dose.
* Store the medicine in a closed container at room temperature, away from heat, moisture, and direct light.
Drugs and Foods to Avoid:
Ask your doctor or pharmacist before using any other medicine, including over-the-counter medicines, vitamins,
and herbal products.
* Some medicines can affect how methocarbamol works. Tell your doctor if you are using pyridostigmine.
* Do not drink alcohol while you are using this medicine.
* Tell your doctor if you use anything else that makes you sleepy. Some examples are allergy medicine, narcotic pain
medicine, and alcohol.
Warnings While Using This Medicine:
Tell your doctor ifyou are pregnant or breastfeeding, or if you have kidney disease, liver disease, or myasthenia
gravis.
This medicine may make you dizzy or drowsy. Do not drive or do anything that could bedangerous until you
know how this medicine affects you.
Tell any doctor or dentist who treats you that you are using this medicine. This medicine may affect certain
medical test results.
Your doctor will check your progress and the effects of this medicine at regular visits. Keep all appointments.
Keep all medicine out of the reach of children. Never share your medicine with anyone.
Possible Side Effects While Using This Medicine:
Call your doctor right away if you notice any of these side effects:
+ Allergic reaction: Itching or hives, swelling inyour face or hands, swelling or tingling inyour mouth or throat,
chest tightness, trouble breathing
Blurred vision, redness, pain, or swelling of the eyes
Dark urine or pale stools, nausea, vomiting, loss of appetite, stomach pain, yellow skin or eyes
Fever
Lightheadedness, dizziness, fainting
Seizures
Slow heartbeat
* Unusual bleeding, bruising, or weakness
If you notice these less serious side effects, talk with your doctor:
* Confusion, trouble sleeping

Attached Information Oxycodone/Acetaminophen (By mouth) (English)
Oxycodone/Acetaminophen (By mouth)
Acetaminophen (a-seet-a-MIN-oh-fen), Oxycodone Hydrochloride (ox-i- KOE-done hye-droe-KLOR-ide)
Treats moderate to moderately severe pain. This medicine is a narcotic pain reliever.
Brand Name(s): Endocet, Nalocet, Percocet, Primlev
There may beother brand names for this medicine.
When This Medicine Should Not Be Used:
This medicine is not right for everyone. Do not use it if you had an allergic reaction to acetaminophen or oxycodone, or
if you have serious breathing problems or paralytic ileus.
How to Use This Medicine:
Capsule, Liquid, Tablet, Long Acting Tablet
Your doctor will tell you how much medicine to use. Do not use more than directed.
An overdose can be dangerous. Follow directions carefully so you do not get too much medicine at one
time.
Oral liquid: Measure the oral liquid medicine with a marked measuring spoon, oral syringe, or medicine cup.
Swallow the extended-release tablet whole. Do not crush, break, or chew it. Do not lick or wet the tablet before
placing it inyour mouth. Do not give this medicine through a feeding tube.
This medicine should come with a Medication Guide. Ask your pharmacist for a copy if you do not have one.
Missed dose: Ifyou miss a dose of this medicine, skip the missed dose and go back to your regular dosing
schedule. Do not double doses.
Store the medicine in a closed container at room temperature, away from heat, moisture, and direct light. Drop off
any unused narcotic medicine at a drug take-back location right away. If you do not have a drug take-back
location near you, flush any unused narcotic medicine down the toilet. Check your local drug store and clinics for
take-back locations. You can also check the DEA web site for locations. Here is the link to the FDAsafe disposal of
medicines website: www.fda.gov/drugs/resourcesforyou/consumers/buyingusingmedicinesafely/
ensuringsafeuseofmedicine/safedisposalofmedicines/ucm186187.htm
Drugs and Foods to Avoid:
Ask your doctor or pharmacist before using any other medicine, including over-the-counter medicines, vitamins,
and herbal products.
* Do not use Xartemis™ XR if you are using or have used an MAO inhibitor in the past 14days.
+ Some medicines can affect how this medicine works. Tell your doctor if you are using any of the following:
©Carbamazepine, erythromycin, ketoconazole, lamotrigine, mirtazapine, naltrexone, phenytoin, propranolol,
rifampin, ritonavir, tramadol, trazodone, or zidovudine
° Birth control pills
©Diuretic (water pill)
° Medicine to treat depression
° Phenothiazine medicine
©Triptan medicine to treat migraine headaches
* Do not drink alcohol while you are using this medicine. Acetaminophen can damage your liver, and alcohol can
increase this risk. Do not take acetaminophen without asking your doctor if you have 3 or more drinks of alcohol
every day.

133
ah
dh Deaconess
Health System
* Tell your doctor if you use anything else that makes you sleepy. Some examples are allergy medicine, narcotic pain
medicine, and alcohol. Tell your doctor if you are using buprenorphine, butorphanol, nalbuphine, pentazocine, a
benzodiazepine, or a muscle relaxer.
Warnings While Using This Medicine:
+ Tell your doctor ifyou are pregnant or breastfeeding, or if you have kidney disease, liver disease, heart disease,
low blood pressure, breathing problems or lung disease (such as asthma, COPD), thyroid problems, Addison
disease, pancreas or gallbladder problems, prostate problems, trouble urinating, or a stomach problems, or a
history of head injury or brain damage, seizures, or alcohol or drug abuse. Tell your doctor if you are allergic to
codeine.
This medicine may cause the following problems:
°
High risk of overdose, which can lead to death
°
Respiratory depression (serious breathing problem that can be life-threatening)
Liver problems ° °
Serious skin reactions
°
Serotonin syndrome (when used with certain medicines)
This medicine may make you dizzy or drowsy. Do not drive or do anything that could bedangerous until you
know how this medicine affects you. Sit or lie down if you feel dizzy. Stand up carefully.
This medicine contains acetaminophen. Read the labels of all other medicines you are using to see if they also
contain acetaminophen, or ask your doctor or pharmacist. Do not use more than 4 grams (4,000 milligrams) total
of acetaminophen inone day.
This medicine can be habit-forming. Do not use more than your prescribed dose. Call your doctor if you think
your medicine is not working.
Do not stop using this medicine suddenly. Your doctor will need to slowly decrease your dose before you stop it
completely.
This medicine could cause infertility. Talk with your doctor before using this medicine if you plan to have children.
This medicine may cause constipation, especially with long-term use. Ask your doctor if you should use a laxative
to prevent and treat constipation.
* Keep all medicine out of the reach of children. Never share your medicine with anyone.
Possible Side Effects While Using This Medicine:
Call your doctor right away if you notice any of these side effects:
Allergic reaction: Itching or hives, swelling inyour face or hands, swelling or tingling inyour mouth or throat,
chest tightness, trouble breathing
Anxiety, restlessness, fast heartbeat, fever, muscle spasms, twitching, diarrhea, seeing or hearing things that are
not there
Blistering, peeling, red skin rash
Blue lips, fingernails, or skin
Dark urine or pale stools, loss of appetite, stomach pain, yellow skin or eyes
Extreme weakness, shallow breathing, uneven heartbeat, seizures, sweating, or cold or clammy skin
Severe confusion, lightheadedness, dizziness, or fainting
Severe constipation, nausea, or vomiting
Trouble breathing or slow breathing
If you notice these less serious side effects, talk with your doctor:
* Headache
* Mild constipation, nausea, or vomiting
* Mild sleepiness or drowsiness

Attached Information Polyethylene Glycol 3350 (By mouth) (English)
Polyethylene Glycol 3350 (By mouth)
Polyethylene Glycol (pol-ee-ETH-i-leen GLYE-kol)
Treats occasional constipation.
Brand Name(s): Equate ClearLax, GaviLAX, Good Neighbor Pharmacy Clear Lax, Good Sense ClearLax, Health
Mart Clearlax, Healthylax Polyethylene Glycol 3350, Leader ClearLax, MiraLAX, Purelax, Sunmark Clearmax,
TopCare ClearLax
There may beother brand names for this medicine.
When This Medicine Should Not Be Used:
You should not use this medicine if you have had an allergic reaction to polyethylene glycol, or if you have signs of a
bowel obstruction (nausea, vomiting, stomach pain or bloating).
How to Use This Medicine:
Powder, Liquid, Packet
Your doctor will tell you how much of this medicine to take and how often. Do not take more medicine or take it
more often than your doctor tells you to. This medicine is not for long-term use.
Always dissolve the powder in a full glass (8 ounces) of water, juice, soda, coffee, or tea before swallowing it. If you
need to measure your medicine, use a marked measuring cup or spoon.
Measure the oral liquid medicine with a marked measuring spoon, oral syringe, or medicine cup.
It may take 2 days or longer for this medicine to help you have a bowel movement.
If a dose is missed:
+ If you miss a dose or forget to take your medicine, take it as soon as you can. If it is almost time for your next
dose, wait until then to take the medicine and skip the missed dose.
* Do not use extra medicine to make up for a missed dose.
How to Store and Dispose of This Medicine:
* Store the medicine at room temperature ina closed container, away from heat, moisture, and direct light.
* Keep all medicine out of the reach of children and never share your medicine with anyone.
Drugs and Foods to Avoid:
Ask your doctor or pharmacist before using any other medicine, including over-the-counter medicines, vitamins,
and herbal products.
Warnings While Using This Medicine:
+ Make sure your doctor knows if you are pregnant or breastfeeding.
* Do not use this medicine longer than 2 weeks unless your doctor has told you to.
Possible Side Effects While Using This Medicine:
Call your doctor right away if you notice any of these side effects:
+ Allergic reaction: Itching or hives, swelling inface or hands, swelling or tingling in the mouth or throat, tightness
in chest, trouble breathing.
* Severe stomach pain, bloating, vomiting, or diarrhea.
If you notice these less serious side effects, talk with your doctor:
* Mild cramps, bloating, diarrhea, or gas.

Attached Information Hip Fracture (General Information) (English)
Hip Fracture
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
What is a hip fracture? A hip fracture is a break inthe upper part of your femur (thigh bone).
Hip and Pelvis
Pelvis
(hip bone)
Hip joint
Femoral
head
Femur
(thigh bane)
What are the signs and symptoms of a hip fracture?
* Pain in your upper thigh, groin, or buttock
* Pain when you flex or rotate your hip
* Trouble placing weight on your leg and walking
* One leg looks shorter than the other
How is a hip fracture diagnosed? X-rays, an MRI, or CT ofyour hip and femur may show a fracture. You may be
given contrast liquid to help the bones show up better in pictures. Tell the healthcare provider ifyou have ever had an
allergic reaction to contrast liquid. Do not enter the MRI room with anything metal. Metal can cause serious injury. Tell
the healthcare provider if you have any metal in or onyour body.
How is a hip fracture treated?
* Medicines may be given to prevent or treat pain. You may also need blood thinners to prevent a blood clot.

What can I do to prevent falls? The following can help you prevent another hip fracture or complications of your hip
fracture:
* Get regular exercise. Include exercises that strengthen your legs and improve your balance. Askabout the best
exercise plan for you.

dh Deaconess Health System
* Talk to your healthcare provider about all of the medicines you take. Some medicines can cause dizziness or
drowsiness and increase your risk for falls.
* Have your vision checked regularly. Your vision may get worse over time and increase your risk for falls.
* Use a walking device, such as a cane or walker, if you have trouble keeping your balance.
* Make your home safe:
° Improve the lighting in your home so that you can see where you are walking.
° Add grab bars to the inside and outside of your tub or shower and next to the toilet.
° Add railings to both sides of your stairways.
° Remove throw rugs and other objects that can cause you to trip and fall.
Remove objects,
like books,
from walkways
Fall Prevention for Adults
What can I do to manage a hip fracture?
* Eat foods that are high in calcium and vitamin D. Your healthcare provider may tell you to eat more dairy
products, such as milk and cheese, for calcium. Spinach, salmon, and dried beans are also good sources of
calcium. Cereal, bread, and orange juice may befortified with vitamin D. You also get vitamin D from exposure to

* Rest as directed. You may need a brace or pillow between your legs while your fracture heals.
* Go to physical therapy as directed. A physical therapist will teach you exercises to help improve movement and
strength, and to decrease pain during recovery.
Call your local emergency number (911 in the US) if:
* Your leg feels warm, tender, and painful. It may look swollen and red.
* You feel lightheaded, short of breath, and have chest pain.
* You cough up blood.
When should I seek immediate care?
* You have severe pain, even after you take pain medicine.
* Your legs are numb.

When should | call my doctor?
* You have afever.
* You haveablister or open sore.
* You have asore that is red, swollen, or draining pus.
* You have increased pain, numbness, tingling, or leg swelling.
* You have worsening function or deformity.
* You have questions or concerns about your condition or care.

treatment options with your healthcare providers to decide what care you want to receive. You always have the right to

Attached Information Methocarbamol (By mouth) (English)
Methocarbamol (By mouth)
Methocarbamol (meth-oh-KAR-ba-mol)
Treats muscle pain and spasms.
Brand Name(s): Robaxin, Robaxin-750
There may beother brand names for this medicine.
When This Medicine Should Not Be Used:
This medicine is not right for everyone. Do not use it if you had an allergic reaction to methocarbamol.
How to Use This Medicine:
Tablet
* Take your medicine as directed. Your dose may need to bechanged several times to find what works best for you.
* Missed dose: Take a dose as soon as you remember. If it is almost time for your next dose, wait until then and
take a regular dose. Do not take extra medicine to make up for a missed dose.
* Store the medicine in a closed container at room temperature, away from heat, moisture, and direct light.
Drugs and Foods to Avoid:
Ask your doctor or pharmacist before using any other medicine, including over-the-counter medicines, vitamins,
and herbal products.
* Some medicines can affect how methocarbamol works. Tell your doctor if you are using pyridostigmine.
* Do not drink alcohol while you are using this medicine.
* Tell your doctor if you use anything else that makes you sleepy. Some examples are allergy medicine, narcotic pain
medicine, and alcohol.
Warnings While Using This Medicine:
Tell your doctor ifyou are pregnant or breastfeeding, or if you have kidney disease, liver disease, or myasthenia
gravis.
This medicine may make you dizzy or drowsy. Do not drive or do anything that could bedangerous until you
know how this medicine affects you.
Tell any doctor or dentist who treats you that you are using this medicine. This medicine may affect certain
medical test results.
Your doctor will check your progress and the effects of this medicine at regular visits. Keep all appointments.
Keep all medicine out of the reach of children. Never share your medicine with anyone.
Possible Side Effects While Using This Medicine:
Call your doctor right away if you notice any of these side effects:
+ Allergic reaction: Itching or hives, swelling inyour face or hands, swelling or tingling inyour mouth or throat,
chest tightness, trouble breathing
Blurred vision, redness, pain, or swelling of the eyes
Dark urine or pale stools, nausea, vomiting, loss of appetite, stomach pain, yellow skin or eyes
Fever
Lightheadedness, dizziness, fainting
Seizures
Slow heartbeat
* Unusual bleeding, bruising, or weakness
If you notice these less serious side effects, talk with your doctor:
* Confusion, trouble sleeping

Attached Information Oxycodone/Acetaminophen (By mouth) (English)
Oxycodone/Acetaminophen (By mouth)
Acetaminophen (a-seet-a-MIN-oh-fen), Oxycodone Hydrochloride (ox-i- KOE-done hye-droe-KLOR-ide)
Treats moderate to moderately severe pain. This medicine is a narcotic pain reliever.
Brand Name(s): Endocet, Nalocet, Percocet, Primlev
There may beother brand names for this medicine.
When This Medicine Should Not Be Used:
This medicine is not right for everyone. Do not use it if you had an allergic reaction to acetaminophen or oxycodone, or
if you have serious breathing problems or paralytic ileus.
How to Use This Medicine:
Capsule, Liquid, Tablet, Long Acting Tablet
Your doctor will tell you how much medicine to use. Do not use more than directed.
An overdose can be dangerous. Follow directions carefully so you do not get too much medicine at one
time.
Oral liquid: Measure the oral liquid medicine with a marked measuring spoon, oral syringe, or medicine cup.
Swallow the extended-release tablet whole. Do not crush, break, or chew it. Do not lick or wet the tablet before
placing it inyour mouth. Do not give this medicine through a feeding tube.
This medicine should come with a Medication Guide. Ask your pharmacist for a copy if you do not have one.
Missed dose: Ifyou miss a dose of this medicine, skip the missed dose and go back to your regular dosing
schedule. Do not double doses.
Store the medicine in a closed container at room temperature, away from heat, moisture, and direct light. Drop off
any unused narcotic medicine at a drug take-back location right away. If you do not have a drug take-back
location near you, flush any unused narcotic medicine down the toilet. Check your local drug store and clinics for
take-back locations. You can also check the DEA web site for locations. Here is the link to the FDAsafe disposal of
medicines website: www.fda.gov/drugs/resourcesforyou/consumers/buyingusingmedicinesafely/
ensuringsafeuseofmedicine/safedisposalofmedicines/ucm186187.htm
Drugs and Foods to Avoid:
Ask your doctor or pharmacist before using any other medicine, including over-the-counter medicines, vitamins,
and herbal products.
* Do not use Xartemis™ XR if you are using or have used an MAO inhibitor in the past 14days.
+ Some medicines can affect how this medicine works. Tell your doctor if you are using any of the following:
©Carbamazepine, erythromycin, ketoconazole, lamotrigine, mirtazapine, naltrexone, phenytoin, propranolol,
rifampin, ritonavir, tramadol, trazodone, or zidovudine
° Birth control pills
©Diuretic (water pill)
° Medicine to treat depression
° Phenothiazine medicine
©Triptan medicine to treat migraine headaches
* Do not drink alcohol while you are using this medicine. Acetaminophen can damage your liver, and alcohol can
increase this risk. Do not take acetaminophen without asking your doctor if you have 3 or more drinks of alcohol
every day.

151
ah
dh Deaconess
Health System
* Tell your doctor if you use anything else that makes you sleepy. Some examples are allergy medicine, narcotic pain
medicine, and alcohol. Tell your doctor if you are using buprenorphine, butorphanol, nalbuphine, pentazocine, a
benzodiazepine, or a muscle relaxer.
Warnings While Using This Medicine:
+ Tell your doctor ifyou are pregnant or breastfeeding, or if you have kidney disease, liver disease, heart disease,
low blood pressure, breathing problems or lung disease (such as asthma, COPD), thyroid problems, Addison
disease, pancreas or gallbladder problems, prostate problems, trouble urinating, or a stomach problems, or a
history of head injury or brain damage, seizures, or alcohol or drug abuse. Tell your doctor if you are allergic to
codeine.
This medicine may cause the following problems:
°
High risk of overdose, which can lead to death
°
Respiratory depression (serious breathing problem that can be life-threatening)
Liver problems ° °
Serious skin reactions
°
Serotonin syndrome (when used with certain medicines)
This medicine may make you dizzy or drowsy. Do not drive or do anything that could bedangerous until you
know how this medicine affects you. Sit or lie down if you feel dizzy. Stand up carefully.
This medicine contains acetaminophen. Read the labels of all other medicines you are using to see if they also
contain acetaminophen, or ask your doctor or pharmacist. Do not use more than 4 grams (4,000 milligrams) total
of acetaminophen inone day.
This medicine can be habit-forming. Do not use more than your prescribed dose. Call your doctor if you think
your medicine is not working.
Do not stop using this medicine suddenly. Your doctor will need to slowly decrease your dose before you stop it
completely.
This medicine could cause infertility. Talk with your doctor before using this medicine if you plan to have children.
This medicine may cause constipation, especially with long-term use. Ask your doctor if you should use a laxative
to prevent and treat constipation.
* Keep all medicine out of the reach of children. Never share your medicine with anyone.
Possible Side Effects While Using This Medicine:
Call your doctor right away if you notice any of these side effects:
Allergic reaction: Itching or hives, swelling inyour face or hands, swelling or tingling inyour mouth or throat,
chest tightness, trouble breathing
Anxiety, restlessness, fast heartbeat, fever, muscle spasms, twitching, diarrhea, seeing or hearing things that are
not there
Blistering, peeling, red skin rash
Blue lips, fingernails, or skin
Dark urine or pale stools, loss of appetite, stomach pain, yellow skin or eyes
Extreme weakness, shallow breathing, uneven heartbeat, seizures, sweating, or cold or clammy skin
Severe confusion, lightheadedness, dizziness, or fainting
Severe constipation, nausea, or vomiting
Trouble breathing or slow breathing
If you notice these less serious side effects, talk with your doctor:
* Headache
* Mild constipation, nausea, or vomiting
* Mild sleepiness or drowsiness

Attached Information Polyethylene Glycol 3350 (By mouth) (English)
Polyethylene Glycol 3350 (By mouth)
Polyethylene Glycol (pol-ee-ETH-i-leen GLYE-kol)
Treats occasional constipation.
Brand Name(s): Equate ClearLax, GaviLAX, Good Neighbor Pharmacy Clear Lax, Good Sense ClearLax, Health
Mart Clearlax, Healthylax Polyethylene Glycol 3350, Leader ClearLax, MiraLAX, Purelax, Sunmark Clearmax,
TopCare ClearLax
There may beother brand names for this medicine.
When This Medicine Should Not Be Used:
You should not use this medicine if you have had an allergic reaction to polyethylene glycol, or if you have signs of a
bowel obstruction (nausea, vomiting, stomach pain or bloating).
How to Use This Medicine:
Powder, Liquid, Packet
Your doctor will tell you how much of this medicine to take and how often. Do not take more medicine or take it
more often than your doctor tells you to. This medicine is not for long-term use.
Always dissolve the powder in a full glass (8 ounces) of water, juice, soda, coffee, or tea before swallowing it. If you
need to measure your medicine, use a marked measuring cup or spoon.
Measure the oral liquid medicine with a marked measuring spoon, oral syringe, or medicine cup.
It may take 2 days or longer for this medicine to help you have a bowel movement.
If a dose is missed:
+ If you miss a dose or forget to take your medicine, take it as soon as you can. If it is almost time for your next
dose, wait until then to take the medicine and skip the missed dose.
* Do not use extra medicine to make up for a missed dose.
How to Store and Dispose of This Medicine:
* Store the medicine at room temperature ina closed container, away from heat, moisture, and direct light.
* Keep all medicine out of the reach of children and never share your medicine with anyone.
Drugs and Foods to Avoid:
Ask your doctor or pharmacist before using any other medicine, including over-the-counter medicines, vitamins,
and herbal products.
Warnings While Using This Medicine:
+ Make sure your doctor knows if you are pregnant or breastfeeding.
* Do not use this medicine longer than 2 weeks unless your doctor has told you to.
Possible Side Effects While Using This Medicine:
Call your doctor right away if you notice any of these side effects:
+ Allergic reaction: Itching or hives, swelling inface or hands, swelling or tingling in the mouth or throat, tightness
in chest, trouble breathing.
* Severe stomach pain, bloating, vomiting, or diarrhea.
If you notice these less serious side effects, talk with your doctor:
* Mild cramps, bloating, diarrhea, or gas.

Attached Information Hip Fracture (General Information) (English)
Hip Fracture
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
What is a hip fracture? A hip fracture is a break inthe upper part of your femur (thigh bone).
Hip and Pelvis
Pelvis
(hip bone)
Hip joint
Femoral
head
Femur
(thigh bane)
What are the signs and symptoms of a hip fracture?
* Pain in your upper thigh, groin, or buttock
* Pain when you flex or rotate your hip
* Trouble placing weight on your leg and walking
* One leg looks shorter than the other
How is a hip fracture diagnosed? X-rays, an MRI, or CT ofyour hip and femur may show a fracture. You may be
given contrast liquid to help the bones show up better in pictures. Tell the healthcare provider ifyou have ever had an
allergic reaction to contrast liquid. Do not enter the MRI room with anything metal. Metal can cause serious injury. Tell
the healthcare provider if you have any metal in or onyour body.
How is a hip fracture treated?
* Medicines may be given to prevent or treat pain. You may also need blood thinners to prevent a blood clot.

* Talk to your healthcare provider about all of the medicines you take. Some medicines can cause dizziness or
drowsiness and increase your risk for falls.
* Have your vision checked regularly. Your vision may get worse over time and increase your risk for falls.
* Use a walking device, such as a cane or walker, if you have trouble keeping your balance.
* Make your home safe:
° Improve the lighting in your home so that you can see where you are walking.
° Add grab bars to the inside and outside of your tub or shower and next to the toilet.
° Add railings to both sides of your stairways.
° Remove throw rugs and other objects that can cause you to trip and fall.
Remove objects,
like books,
from walkways
Fall Prevention for Adults
What can I do to manage a hip fracture?
* Eat foods that are high in calcium and vitamin D. Your healthcare provider may tell you to eat more dairy
products, such as milk and cheese, for calcium. Spinach, salmon, and dried beans are also good sources of
calcium. Cereal, bread, and orange juice may befortified with vitamin D. You also get vitamin D from exposure to

* Rest as directed. You may need a brace or pillow between your legs while your fracture heals.
* Go to physical therapy as directed. A physical therapist will teach you exercises to help improve movement and
strength, and to decrease pain during recovery.
Call your local emergency number (911 in the US) if:
* Your leg feels warm, tender, and painful. It may look swollen and red.
* You feel lightheaded, short of breath, and have chest pain.
* You cough up blood.
When should I seek immediate care?
* You have severe pain, even after you take pain medicine.
* Your legs are numb.

When should | call my doctor?
* You have afever.
* You haveablister or open sore.
* You have asore that is red, swollen, or draining pus.
* You have increased pain, numbness, tingling, or leg swelling.
* You have worsening function or deformity.
* You have questions or concerns about your condition or care.

treatment options with your healthcare providers to decide what care you want to receive. You always have the right to
refuse treatment. The above information is aneducational aid only. It is not intended as medical advice for individual

I understand that I may need to have one or more blood transfusions. Having ablood transfusion
means receiving blood or blood products donated bysomeone else. Another way to have a

done besides having a blood transfusion.
Problems can occur
Tunderstand that receiving blood or blood products could cause a health problem for me. Blood

can prevent all problems related to blood transfusions. My doctor explained tome the risks of
receiving blood, and I amsatisfied with that explanation. He or she also explained that I could
die or have otherbadoutcomes ifI choose not toreceive blood.

Point: Pain Rating Scale (Not Started)
Learning Progress Summary
Patient Friendly Description: Medication can be very helpful, but some pain medicines are more effective in controlling pain when they
are combined with other methods of treatment. Here are a few things we recommend. If you're able, do not stay in bed all day. Spend
some of the day in the chair and walk around your room and in the hall. Be sure to ask for help if you are not steady on your feet. Try
using ice packs whcih are available upon request if apropriate for your care. Deep breathing and visualizing your pain leaving your
body can be helpful. Mindfulness and meditation also addresses other emotions such as anxiety and fear. Splint with a pillow when
you cough, deep breathe, or when repositioning after surgery or a procedure.
Description:
Provide medication specific handouts when appropriate.
Description:
Provide patient with written information on the Pain Rating Scale, Explain the rating scale of 0 to 10 (both verbal and non-verbal) and its
purpose.
Patient Friendly Description: You will experience post-operative pain and discomfort. Patients are encouraged to contact staff for pain
medication before the pain becomes intolerable or if the pain medication is not working. Controlling your pain is a key factor to
effectively participate in your therapy.
Description:
Give patient/caregiver written information about safety devices being used, if applicable. Explain reason for use.
Patient Friendly Description: I was provided with written information on the Pain Rating Scale. This information is included in "My
Patient Guide"as well as my Joint Replacement Guidebook.
Patient Friendly Description: For patient safety, you may see different pieces of equipment in your room. This could include gait belts,
walkers, wheelchairs and other pieces of equipment if applicable.
Description:
Explain to patient/family about other ways of controlling pain than medications. The following are suggestions: position change,
aromatherapy, deep slow breathing exercises, decrease environmental stimuli,distraction, quiet environment, guided imagery,
Description:
Encourage the patient to contact staff for pain medication before the pain becomes intolerable or if pain medication is not working.

Patient Friendly Description: The Social Worker or Case Manager will discuss with you any discharge needs that you may have. If
you are going home, someone will need to drive you there. However, if you are going to rehab or a skilled nursing facility,
transportation will be arranged for you. Therapists and nurses will review with you throughout your stay any restrictions that you may
have. You can review your activity restrictions and any special precautions in your Joint Replacement Guidebook.
Description:
Instruct patient/family to call the doctor if there is an increased temperature (greater than 100.8), unrelieved pain, symptoms not
relieved or worsening, chest pain, shortness of breath, side effects of medications or any change in condition.
Description:
Explain that the patient's armband, and two identifiers will be checked before medications are given to the patient.
Patient Friendly Description: Call your doctor if you have a fever greater than 100.8 °F; signs of infection; unrelieved pain; symptoms
that are not relieved or worsening; chest pain or shortness of breath; or any change in your condition.
Explain why there is a higher risk of falls in the hospital. Review ways to reduce the chance of falling. Explain safe room set up.
Description:
Description:
Help patient identify additional resources. Refer to Social Services/Pastoral Care/Case Management, if necessary.
Patient Friendly Description: When administering medications the nurse will scan your armband and check two identifiers before
medications are given to you for your safety.
Description:
Patient Friendly Description: Your nurse can help you get in contact with your Case Manager or Social Worker if you have questions
about additional resources that may be necessary. Your Joint Replacement Guidebook also has a list of Community Resources.
Review the discharge plan with patient/family including follow-up appointments, diet, activity, medications and special precautions.
Patient Friendly Description: I was provided with written information on Fall Prevention. This information is included in "My Patient
Guide".

Title: General Surgery (Done)
Topic: Post Operative Teaching (Done)
Point: Resume Activity (Done)
Point: Post Operative Pain (Done)
Laproscopic: Explain to the patient/family that during the procedure, carbon dioxide gas was introduced into your abdomen to allow for
visualization of your organs. A very small amount of gas will remain in your body and is naturally absorbed and/or
expelled. You may notice your abdomen is bloated due to the remaining gas. Pain in your shoulders, neck, or abdomen may be
experienced for the next couple of days. The best way to expel this excess gas is ambulation.
Open: Explain to the patient/family that there will be pain at the incision site. Instruct patient regarding splinting incision with pillow for
turning, coughing, and deep breathing, and getting out of bed. Explain to patient pain in your abdomen may
be experienced due to the movement of gas. The best way to expel this gas is ambulation.
Learner
Progress:
Not documented in this visit.
Point: Resuming Intake (Done)
Give explanation on how and when to resume/progress diet.
Instruct learners on name and purpose of medications and possible side effects. If appropriate, include food/drug interactions, reporting
of efficacy, and symptoms to report.
Description:
Provide information regarding post op pain control. Discuss the pain scale (1-10) and currently ordered pain medication.
Description:
Description:
Give explanation on when to resume/progress activity.
Patient Friendly Description: Your nurse will inform you on how and when to resume/progress your diet following the
procedure/surgery.
Patient Friendly Description: Your nurse will inform you on how and when to resume/progress your activity following the
procedure/surgery, including any specific limitations or restrictions that should be followed.
Patient Friendly Description: "Caregivers may give you medicine to take away or decrease your pain. Do not wait until the pain is
severe to ask for your medicine. Tell caregivers if your pain does not decrease. The medicine may not work as well at controlling your
pain if you wait too long to take it. Pain medicine can make you dizzy or sleepy. Prevent falls by calling a caregiver when you want to
get out of bed or if you need help.
If you had a procedure/surgery that was done laparoscopically, carbon dioxide gas was introduced into your abdomen to allow for
visualization of your organs. A very small amount of gas will remain in your body and is naturally absorbed and/or
expelled. You may notice your abdomen is bloated due to the remaining gas and the best way to expel this excess gas is by increased
activity, such as walking with assistance in the halls. Pain in your shoulders, neck, or abdomen may be experienced for the next
couple of days.
If you had a procedure/surgery that required an open incision, there will be pain at the incision site. Splinting the incision with a pillow
when turning, coughing, and deep breathing, and getting out of bed can help alleviate the pain. Pain in your abdomen may
be experienced due to the movement of gas and the best way to expel this gas is by increased activity, such as walking with
assistance in the halls."
Description:

Point: Post Operative Drain/Incision Care (Done)
dry. Monitor for signs and symptoms of infection including redness, firmness, change in amount, color, or odor of drainage, open areas of
incision, protecting incision from injury, and dressing changes.
Point: Prevention of Post Operative Complications (Done)
activity and ambulation, including turning frequently while in bed, sitting up at meal times, and ambulating in hall. These interventions can
decrease likelihood of skin breakdown, increase comfort, and promote return of bowel function.
Learning Progress Summary
Patient Friendly Description: "Your nurse will educate you on the care of any incision(s) and the surrounding skin, including ways to
protect the incision by keeping it clean and dry and performing dressing changes. Some common signs and symptoms of infection to
monitor for, include: pain, redness or swelling at the incision; a change in the amount, color or odor of drainage.
If you have any post-operative drains, your nurse will inform you of their purpose and educate you on specific care instructions and
how to recognize changes in amount and type of drainage from the drain. "
Explain purpose/use of incentive spirometer. Indicate reason for using incentive is to exercise the lungs to prevent pneumonia and
other respiratory complications. Explain purpose/use of teds and scds is to decrease risk of DVT. Encourage progressive
Learning Progress Summary
Patient Friendly Description: You may be given an incentive spirometer, which is used to exercise the lungs to prevent pneumonia and
other respiratory complications. You may also have supplies/equipment such as TED hose or SCDs used to decrease the risk of a
Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT), or a blood clot that forms in a deep vein of the body. TED hose are stockings that help prevent blood
clots and swelling in your legs. Sequential Compression Devices (SCDs) are inflatable sleeves used to improve blood flow. You may
be encouraged to perform interventions that can decrease the likelihood of skin breakdown, increase comfort and promote the return
of bowel function, including: turning frequently while in bed, sitting up at meal times, and ambulating in the halls.
Description:
Description:
Explain purpose of drain and instruct on care. Teach patient how to recognize changes in amount and type of drainage from drain.
Explain care of incision as well as peri-wound skin, including not touching the incision, keeping the incision clean and

Patient Name: Kimberly K Brasher

Discharge Date: 3/12/2023

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSES
FOCUSED ISSUES:
Active Hospital Problems
Iron deficiency anemia
Reactive thrombocytosis
Hypokalemia
Medication overdose, accidental or unintentional, initial encounter
Left intertrochanteric fracture status post ORIF [DOB-290]
Consults:
None
Procedures/Interventions/Operations:
None
Reason for hospitalization (as dictated at the time of admission)
HPI altered mental status
Kimberly K Brasher is a 49 y.o. female with PMH of closed left intertrochanteric fracture s/p ORIF [DOB-289],
chronic iron-deficiency anemia, gastric bypass surgery, migraine headaches who was brought to the ED after
being found unresponsive by her daughter at home. Daughter reports that patient received Norco 5.0 mg x 20
tabs on 3/7. She had a follow up with her orthopedic surgeon yesterday (3/9) and received an additional 28
tablets of Norco 7.5 mg. Daughter believes that patient took all of these since getting her prescription filled.
 Per ED documentation patient was awake and alert with GCS 15 on arrival but slow to respond with slurred
Tue Feb 20, 2024 1:29 PM

Condition
--

IP Encounter Report
Brasher, Kimberly K
MRN: 469590, DOB: 3/25/1973, Sex: F
Adm: 3/10/2023, D/C: 3/12/2023
Discharge Summary - Discharge Summary (continued)
Discharge Summary by Vasser-Smith, Montubua, MD at 03/12/23 1137 (continued)
speech. She did not require Narcan.
Vitals on arrival notable for pulse 155. Notable labs: Hemoglobin 7.5, INR 2.0, AST 48, TSH 5.80 with normal
free T4, acetaminophen level 41. CT head and CT cervical spine negative for acute process. X-ray of the left
hip showed intact hardware. Chest x-ray was negative. ED physician discussed case with poison control and
patient was started on NAC protocol in addition to Protonix 40 mg and 1 L normal saline bolus. Hospitalist
service was contacted for admission.
Hospital Course:
Kimberly K BrasherN was admitted to the ICU in guarded condition. She was started on NAC
protocol. Her AST returned to normal levels and her acetaminophen level was undetectable by the next
morning. Her INR fell from 2.0 down to 1.4, hemoglobin remains stable at her baseline of
approximately 7-7.5. I do recommend she follow-up with Hematology with her history of iron deficient
anemia. Patient had completed her NAC, and given all numbers returned to normal range, it was
discontinued
Patient adamantly denies any form of self-harm, appears to be quite embarrassed for the events that
happened. She is unsure how she became so confused and overdosed on the medication. She is
eager to be able to get back home, understands she will not have opiates to help control her pain, and
this does bring her some anxiety. Chart review shows previous visits to Psychiatry where notes do not
reveal any prior SI/HI. Recommendation is to follow-up outpatient psychiatry. Patient was offered
Ultram at discharge but she declined.
PT did recommend inpatient rehab however the patient is adamant that she does not want facility level
rehab and will follow-up as outpatient with her previous order for PT.
Hypokalemia was noted on the morning of 3/11. Potassium 2.8. Patient was given oral and IV
replacement. On the day of discharge potassium has normalized at 3.8.
Physical Exam:
GENERAL: Frail appearing white female in no acute distress.
CHEST: Clear to auscultation bilaterally without wheezes, stridor, crackles. Adequate inspiratory effort and air
flow. No accessory muscle use with breathing.
CARDIAC: Regular S1-S2. No murmur, rub, or gallop. No JVD, cyanosis, or leg edema.
ABDOMEN: Bowel sounds are audible. Soft, nontender, nondistended. No hepatosplenomegaly.
MUSCULOSKELETAL: S/p left hip ORIF, incision c/d/i
SKIN: Warm and dry. No rashes.
NEUROLOGIC: Awake and alert speech is clear quite conversant. 5/5 strength in bilateral upper and lower
extremities. Cranial nerves 2-12 are intact. No gross sensory deficits.
PSYCHIATRIC: Alert and oriented x3. Pleasant mood and affect.
Condition upon Discharge: Improved
Disposition: Home with outpatient PT
Instructions for follow up care:

There is mild wedging of the C4 vertebral body anteriorly. This is age

Impression:
IMPRESSION:
Mild anterior wedging of the C4 vertebral body, which is age indeterminate.

for trauma, MRI can be obtained to assess for the presence of edema.

Editor: Jones, William T, MD (Physician)
 .
CHIEF COMPLAINT
Chief Complaint
Patient presents with
Drug Overdose
HPI
Kimberly K Brasher is a 49 y.o. female who presents with last known well of 8:00 p.m. last night. She had left
hip surgery 2 weeks ago. She was prescribed new prescription of Norco yesterday. Her daughter filled it. At
2:30 a.m. this morning daughter noticed that she was confused thinking that she was working in nursing home.
She then fell in her bedroom. Patient is confused and altered. Disoriented answers appropriate age and
name. Confabulates story though from patient. She denies pain anywhere other than her left hip. Patient
denies any suicide ideation or self-harm ideation. She states she is not take anything to harm herself. Patient
states that she thinks this is due to the medication. Daughter notes that her recent Norco bottles are
completely emptying they were filled yesterday. She is also prescribed Flexeril and Xarelto to prevent blood
clots. Denies alcohol or illicit drugs. Daughter states no concern for self-harm.
Estimate she took 28 tablets of norco 7.5-325 between 8pm and 2:30am: That is 9100mg tylenol, 150mg/kg =
8550mg is toxic dose. Poison control notified
REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Twelve point review of systems obtained and otherwise unremarkable except as above.

Procalcitonin Normals
GENERAL COMMENTS Antibiotic initiation should not be withheld for patients with
sepsis or
pneumonia with radiographic evidence of infiltrates. A low procalcitonin should prompt
the
investigation of non-bacterial or non-infectious causes of patient clinical status. Repeat
low
 or declining procalcitonin (>80% from peak) may indicate an opportunity for antibiotic
de-escalation or discontinuation. Low procalcitonin levels in the presence of acute
bronchitis,
 COPD exacerbations, and other related conditions generally indicates the absence of
clinically
 significant bacterial infections. Please refer to the procalcitonin guidance document on
DWeb

The initial differential diagnosis for this patient's presentation include: Intracranial hemorrhage, polypharmacy,
accidental overdose
Each of the above mentioned unique tests were ordered by myself for workup of this patient.
I have reviewed the external records available to me, pertaining to this patient's care.
I have reviewed the results of each unique tests mentioned above.
The Independent Historian, daughter, was used to gather history pertaining to this patient's care.
My Independent Interpretation of this patient's history, exam and workup (including laboratory, radiology or
ancillary testing) are most consistent with medication overdose accidental along with concern for toxic
ingestion of Tylenol in her Norco. Resulting altered mental status. No intracranial hemorrhage. Unknown time
of ingestion thus will treat empirically with N-acetylcysteine IV protocol. No blood in the stool. Anemia for last

Impression and plan was discussed with patient, nursing and daughter . All questions were answered.
HISTORY
History obtained from: chart review and the patient
Kimberly K Brasher is a 49 y.o. female with PMH of closed left intertrochanteric fracture s/p ORIF [DOB-140],
chronic iron-deficiency anemia, gastric bypass surgery, migraine headaches who was brought to the ED after
being found unresponsive by her daughter at home. Daughter reports that patient received Norco 5.0 mg x 20
tabs on 3/7. She had a follow up with her orthopedic surgeon yesterday (3/9) and received an additional 28
tablets of Norco 7.5 mg. Daughter believes that patient took all of these since getting her prescription filled.
Per ED documentation patient was awake and alert with GCS 15 on arrival but slow to respond with slurred
speech. She did not require Narcan.
Vitals on arrival notable for pulse 155. Notable labs: Hemoglobin 7.5, INR 2.0, AST 48, TSH 5.80 with normal
free T4, acetaminophen level 41. CT head and CT cervical spine negative for acute process. X-ray of the left
hip showed intact hardware. Chest x-ray was negative. ED physician discussed case with poison control and
patient was started on NAC protocol in addition to Protonix 40 mg and 1 L normal saline bolus. Hospitalist
service was contacted for admission.
Review of Systems: A complete 10 system review of systems was completed and negative except as noted in
the HPI or here.
Concurrent Medical/ Past Medical History
Past Medical History:
Diagnosis Date
Anemia
HGSIL on cytologic smear of cervix
Migraine
Past Surgical History
 has a past surgical history that includes gastric bypass surgery (2008); breast enhancement surgery (2012);
cholecystectomy (2005); foot surgery; and femur fracture surgery (Left, 2/26/2023).
Home Medications
No outpatient medications have been marked as taking for the 3/10/23 encounter
(Hospital Encounter).
Allergies
Allergies
Allergen Reactions
Penicillins

H&P by Walker, William M, DO at 03/10/23 1032 (continued)
Procalcitonin Normals [DOB-240] Final
 Value:GENERAL COMMENTS Antibiotic initiation should not be withheld for patients with
sepsis or
pneumonia with radiographic evidence of infiltrates. A low procalcitonin should prompt the
investigation of non-bacterial or non-infectious causes of patient clinical status. Repeat low
 or declining procalcitonin (>80% from peak) may indicate an opportunity for antibiotic
de-escalation or discontinuation. Low procalcitonin levels in the presence of acute bronchitis,
 COPD exacerbations, and other related conditions generally indicates the absence of clinically
 significant bacterial infections. Please refer to the procalcitonin guidance document on DWeb
 under Quick Links - Antimicrobial Stewardship for additional information.

IP Encounter Report
Brasher, Kimberly K
MRN: 469590, DOB: 3/25/1973, Sex: F

BestPractice Advisories Brasher, Kimberly K [469590]
Progress Notes - Progress Notes
Progress Notes by Nesmith, Talia J, MD at 03/11/23 0804
Author: Nesmith, Talia J, MD Service: Physician to Physician Author Type: Physician
Filed: 03/11/23 1413 Date of Service: 03/11/23 0804 Status: Signed
Editor: Nesmith, Talia J, MD (Physician)
.
Deaconess Progress Note
Patient Name: Kimberly K Brasher
MRN: 469590
DOB: 3/25/1973
Date of Admission: 3/10/2023
Date of Service: 3/11/2023
PMD: No primary care provider on file.
Reason for hospitalization
Kimberly K Brasher is a 49 y.o. female with PMH of closed left intertrochanteric fracture s/p ORIF [DOB-204],
chronic iron-deficiency anemia, gastric bypass surgery, migraine headaches who was brought to the ED after
being found unresponsive by her daughter at home. Daughter reports that patient received Norco 5.0 mg x 20
tabs on 3/7. She had a follow up with her orthopedic surgeon yesterday (3/9) and received an additional 28
tablets of Norco 7.5 mg. Daughter believes that patient took all of these since getting her prescription filled.
 Per ED documentation patient was awake and alert with GCS 15 on arrival but slow to respond with slurred
speech. She did not require Narcan.
Vitals on arrival notable for pulse 155. Notable labs: Hemoglobin 7.5, INR 2.0, AST 48, TSH 5.80 with normal
free T4, acetaminophen level 41. CT head and CT cervical spine negative for acute process. X-ray of the left
hip showed intact hardware. Chest x-ray was negative. ED physician discussed case with poison control and
patient was started on NAC protocol in addition to Protonix 40 mg and 1 L normal saline bolus. Hospitalist
service was contacted for admission.
Hospital course
Patient was admitted to the ICU in guarded condition. She was started on NAC protocol. Her AST
returned to normal levels and her acetaminophen level was undetectable by the next morning. Her INR
fell from 2.0 down to 1.4, hemoglobin remains stable at her baseline of approximately 7-7.5. I do
recommend she follow-up with Hematology with her history of iron deficient anemia. Patient had
completed her NAC, and given all numbers returned to normal range, it was discontinued
Patient adamantly denies any form of self-harm, appears to be quite embarrassed for the events that
happened. She is unsure how she became so confused and overdosed on the medication. She is
eager to be able to get back home, understands she will not have opiates to help control her pain, and
this does bring her some anxiety. Chart review shows previous visits to Psychiatry where notes do not
reveal any prior SI/HI. Recommendation is to follow-up outpatient psychiatry.
Hypokalemia was noted on the morning of 3/11. Potassium 2.8. Patient was given oral and IV

PHYSICAL THERAPY
Patient: Kimberly K Brasher
Age: 49 y.o.
DOB: 3/25/1973
Narrative
Patient admitted after an accidental overdose of pain meds. Patient fractured her left hip 2 weeks ago and
underwent ORIF 2/2/6/23.
Patient lives with her adult child and currently walking short distances with a rolling walker. Prior to hip fx
patient was independent.
Patient's activity is limited by pain and weakness. Motivated to get up and walk to commode with assist and
walker.
Recommendations
Therapy Recommendations: Pt would benefit from continued therapy at a facility level
Outcome Measure
Basic Mobility Raw Score: 17 (03/11/23 1057)
Basic Mobility T-Scale Score: 39.67 (03/11/23 1057)
Plan
Treatment/Interventions: Transfer training, Gait training, Bed mobility
PT Frequency: 5x/week, 7x/week
Prior Level of Function
Permanent Residence: Home

Hypoglycemia Treatment Oral (15:15 Rule)
Oral Treatment Options 15 Grams FAST Carbohydrates
Snack (if meal greater than 1 hour away)
Fast Carbohydrates 240 ml skim milk
120 ml unsweetened applejuice (chose for Renal Patients or elevated
K+)
120 ml regular soda
Snack for meal greater than 1 hour away 1 slice bread / toast
6 saltines (avoid in renal or low sodium diets)
3 squares of graham crackers
For Non-Renal Patient: 1 Tablespoon peanut butter (protein) may be
added to any of the above

Procalcitonin 0.07 <0.10 NG/ML DHND
Comment:
====================
LOWER RESPIRATORY TRACT INFECTION
Antibiotics are strongly discouraged. Recommend repeat PCT if antibiotics not
 given and not improved in 6-12 hours.
====================
SEPSIS
Antibiotics are strongly discouraged.
Antibiotic INITIATION should not be withheld if highly suspicious for sepsis.
Recommend repeat PCT if antibiotics not given and not improved in 6-12 hours.
====================
Procalcitonin Normals -- DHND
Result:
GENERAL COMMENTS Antibiotic initiation should not be withheld for patients with sepsis or
pneumonia with radiographic evidence of infiltrates. A low procalcitonin should prompt the
investigation of non-bacterial or non-infectious causes of patient clinical status. Repeat low
 or declining procalcitonin (>80% from peak) may indicate an opportunity for antibiotic
de-escalation or discontinuation. Low procalcitonin levels in the presence of acute bronchitis,
 COPD exacerbations, and other related conditions generally indicates the absence of clinically
 significant bacterial infections. Please refer to the procalcitonin guidance document on DWeb
 under Quick Links - Antimicrobial Stewardship for additional information.
Reference Range Flag Lab
Salicylate Level <1.0 <20 MG/DL DHND

XR PELVIS W LATERAL HIP LEFT [314411487] Resulted: 03/10/23 1658, Result status: Final result
Ordering provider: Jones, William T, MD 03/10/23 0820 Resulted by: Morozov, Pavel, MD
Performed: 03/10/23 0858 - 03/10/23 0911 Accession number: DHHND5987878
Resulting lab: RAD/CARD
Narrative:
Indication:
Fall.
Recent hardware within the left neck.
COMPARISON:
[DOB-209].
FINDINGS:
There is left hip hardware, which appears to be grossly intact. The
intertrochanteric fracture is still visualized. There is moderate comminution.
When comparing with CT from [DOB-210], the examination is likely unchanged.
There does not appear to be any new fracture or dislocation.
Impression:
IMPRESSION:
Intact hardware following intramedullary nail placement within the left femur.
Overall, comminuted intertrochanteric fracture appears to be similar.
CT CERVICAL SPINE WO CONTRAST [314411491] Resulted: 03/10/23 1655, Result status: Final result
Ordering provider: Jones, William T, MD 03/10/23 0820 Resulted by: Morozov, Pavel, MD
Performed: 03/10/23 0849 - 03/10/23 0853 Accession number: DHHND5987868
Resulting lab: RAD/CARD
Narrative:
Indication:
altered mental status.
Fall on Xarelto.
COMPARISON:
None.
TECHNIQUE:
Axial images from the base of the skull through the thoracic inlet were
performed followed by 2D multiplanar reformats.
FINDINGS:
There is mild wedging of the C4 vertebral body anteriorly. This is age
indeterminate. Sagittal and coronal alignment are otherwise intact. The
remainder of the vertebral body heights are intact. Intervertebral disc spaces
are preserved.
Impression:
IMPRESSION:
Mild anterior wedging of the C4 vertebral body, which is age indeterminate.
This is favored to be chronic. However, if there is further clinical concern
for trauma, MRI can be obtained to assess for the presence of edema.
CT HEAD WO CONTRAST [314411490] Resulted: 03/10/23 1008, Result status: Final result
Ordering provider: Jones, William T, MD 03/10/23 0820 Resulted by: Cheema, Jugesh, MD

Resulting lab: RAD/CARD
Narrative:
Indication:
Fall.
On blood thinners.
Altered mental status.
TECHNIQUE:

followed by 2D multiplanar reformats.

Admin Instructions: Use the following steps to administer via IV push:
1. Reconstitute powder with 10 ml of saline, the resulting concentration
is 4 mg/ml.
2. Withdraw dose into syringe and administer over at least 2 minutes.
3. If you must administer through line with incompatible drips: - flush line with 5 ml of saline through lowest port
- administer through lowest port over 2 minutes - flush line with 5 ml of saline

checks.

and confidential support for people in distress, prevention and
crisis resources for you or your loved ones, and best practices
for professionals.

Topic: Safety (Done)

Point: Pain Management Alternatives (Done)
heat and cold, laughter, massage, music, physical therapy, and touch therapy. Discuss any previous experiences with pain, and things

Point: Pain Control (Done)
Point: Pain Rating Scale (Done)
Provide medication specific handouts when appropriate.
Learning Progress Summary
Learning Progress Summary
Description:
Encourage the patient to contact staff for pain medication before the pain becomes intolerable or if pain medication is not working.
Description:
Provide patient with written information on the Pain Rating Scale, Explain the rating scale of 0 to 10 (both verbal and non-verbal) and its
purpose.
Description:
Explain to patient/family about other ways of controlling pain than medications. The following are suggestions: position change,
aromatherapy, deep slow breathing exercises, decrease environmental stimuli,distraction, quiet environment, guided imagery,
Patient Friendly Description: Medication can be very helpful, but some pain medicines are more effective in controlling pain when they
are combined with other methods of treatment. Here are a few things we recommend. If you're able, do not stay in bed all day. Spend
some of the day in the chair and walk around your room and in the hall. Be sure to ask for help if you are not steady on your feet. Try
using ice packs whcih are available upon request if apropriate for your care. Deep breathing and visualizing your pain leaving your
body can be helpful. Mindfulness and meditation also addresses other emotions such as anxiety and fear. Splint with a pillow when
you cough, deep breathe, or when repositioning after surgery or a procedure.
Patient Friendly Description: I was provided with written information on the Pain Rating Scale. This information is included in "My
Patient Guide"as well as my Joint Replacement Guidebook.
Learning Progress Summary
Patient Friendly Description: You will experience post-operative pain and discomfort. Patients are encouraged to contact staff for pain
medication before the pain becomes intolerable or if the pain medication is not working. Controlling your pain is a key factor to
effectively participate in your therapy.
Learning Progress Summary
Patient Friendly Description: I was provided information on my room phone number and unit manager phone number. Patients need
to stay on their assigned hospital unit for their health and safety. Choosing to leave the assigned hospital unit, other than for ordered
tests or treatment could result in serious harm to you. All tobacco products and e-cigarettes are not allowed on Deaconess property.
Learning Progress Summary
Description:

Learning Progress Summary
Description:
Review the discharge plan with patient/family including follow-up appointments, diet, activity, medications and special precautions.
Description:
Patient Friendly Description: For patient safety, you may see different pieces of equipment in your room. This could include gait belts,
walkers, wheelchairs and other pieces of equipment if applicable.
Patient Friendly Description: The Social Worker or Case Manager will discuss with you any discharge needs that you may have. If
you are going home, someone will need to drive you there. However, if you are going to rehab or a skilled nursing facility,
transportation will be arranged for you. Therapists and nurses will review with you throughout your stay any restrictions that you may
have. You can review your activity restrictions and any special precautions in your Joint Replacement Guidebook.
Learning Progress Summary
Description:
Explain that the patient's armband, and two identifiers will be checked before medications are given to the patient.
Description:
Description:
Help patient identify additional resources. Refer to Social Services/Pastoral Care/Case Management, if necessary.
Explain why there is a higher risk of falls in the hospital. Review ways to reduce the chance of falling. Explain safe room set up.
Learning Progress Summary
Patient Friendly Description: When administering medications the nurse will scan your armband and check two identifiers before
medications are given to you for your safety.
Patient Friendly Description: Your nurse can help you get in contact with your Case Manager or Social Worker if you have questions
about additional resources that may be necessary. Your Joint Replacement Guidebook also has a list of Community Resources.
Learning Progress Summary
Learning Progress Summary
Patient Friendly Description: I was provided with written information on Fall Prevention. This information is included in "My Patient
Guide".
