Point: Post Operative Pain (Not Started)
Laproscopic: Explain to the patient/family that during the procedure, carbon dioxide gas was introduced into your abdomen to allow for
visualization of your organs. A very small amount of gas will remain in your body and is naturally absorbed and/or
expelled. You may notice your abdomen is bloated due to the remaining gas. Pain in your shoulders, neck, or abdomen may be
experienced for the next couple of days. The best way to expel this excess gas is ambulation.
Open: Explain to the patient/family that there will be pain at the incision site. Instruct patient regarding splinting incision with pillow for
turning, coughing, and deep breathing, and getting out of bed. Explain to patient pain in your abdomen may
be experienced due to the movement of gas. The best way to expel this gas is ambulation.
Point: Naloxone HCl (Deleted)

of efficacy, and symptoms to report.

procedure/surgery.

Point: Post Operative Drain/Incision Care (Not Started)
dry. Monitor for signs and symptoms of infection including redness, firmness, change in amount, color, or odor of drainage, open areas of

expelled. You may notice your abdomen is bloated due to the remaining gas and the best way to expel this excess gas is by increased
activity, such as walking with assistance in the halls. Pain in your shoulders, neck, or abdomen may be experienced for the next
couple of days.
If you had a procedure/surgery that required an open incision, there will be pain at the incision site. Splinting the incision with a pillow
when turning, coughing, and deep breathing, and getting out of bed can help alleviate the pain. Pain in your abdomen may
be experienced due to the movement of gas and the best way to expel this gas is by increased activity, such as walking with
assistance in the halls."
Description:
Explain purpose/use of incentive spirometer. Indicate reason for using incentive is to exercise the lungs to prevent pneumonia and
other respiratory complications. Explain purpose/use of teds and scds is to decrease risk of DVT. Encourage progressive
Description:
Explain purpose of drain and instruct on care. Teach patient how to recognize changes in amount and type of drainage from drain.
Explain care of incision as well as peri-wound skin, including not touching the incision, keeping the incision clean and
Patient Friendly Description: "Caregivers may give you medicine to take away or decrease your pain. Do not wait until the pain is
severe to ask for your medicine. Tell caregivers if your pain does not decrease. The medicine may not work as well at controlling your
pain if you wait too long to take it. Pain medicine can make you dizzy or sleepy. Prevent falls by calling a caregiver when you want to
get out of bed or if you need help.
If you had a procedure/surgery that was done laparoscopically, carbon dioxide gas was introduced into your abdomen to allow for
visualization of your organs. A very small amount of gas will remain in your body and is naturally absorbed and/or
Patient Friendly Description: "Your nurse will educate you on the care of any incision(s) and the surrounding skin, including ways to
protect the incision by keeping it clean and dry and performing dressing changes. Some common signs and symptoms of infection to
monitor for, include: pain, redness or swelling at the incision; a change in the amount, color or odor of drainage.
If you have any post-operative drains, your nurse will inform you of their purpose and educate you on specific care instructions and
how to recognize changes in amount and type of drainage from the drain. "
Patient Friendly Description: You may be given an incentive spirometer, which is used to exercise the lungs to prevent pneumonia and
other respiratory complications. You may also have supplies/equipment such as TED hose or SCDs used to decrease the risk of a
Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT), or a blood clot that forms in a deep vein of the body. TED hose are stockings that help prevent blood
clots and swelling in your legs. Sequential Compression Devices (SCDs) are inflatable sleeves used to improve blood flow. You may
be encouraged to perform interventions that can decrease the likelihood of skin breakdown, increase comfort and promote the return
of bowel function, including: turning frequently while in bed, sitting up at meal times, and ambulating in the halls.

Radiology Results
CT HIP LEFT WO CONTRAST [316373452] Resulted: 04/14/23 1337, Result status: Final result
Resulted by: Morozov, Pavel, MD Performed: 04/14/23 1055 - 04/14/23 1100
Accession number: DHHND6064840 Resulting lab: RAD/CARD
Narrative:
INDICATION:
Left hip pain after IM nail.
COMPARISON:
X-ray of the pelvis from 3/10/2023
TECHNIQUE:
Axial images through the left hip were performed followed by 2D multiplanar
reformats.
FINDINGS:
There is a left IM rod with a dynamic screw. Hardware appears to be intact.
There is redemonstrated intertrochanteric fracture with displacement. Some
callous formation is noted but fracture lucencies are still visualized.
Alignment appears to be intact as does the hardware. The left hip itself
appears to be intact as well.
IMPRESSIONS:
1. Redemonstrated comminuted left intratrochanteric fracture following ORIF.
Hardware is intact. Some healing is noted with fracture lucency still present.
2. The left hip itself is unremarkable. No specific finding is noted to
account for patient's symptoms.
CT HIP LEFT WO CONTRAST [316373452] Resulted: 04/14/23 1217, Result status: Preliminary result

Point: Pain Rating Scale (Not Started)
Point: Fall Prevention (Not Started)
Patient Friendly Description: You will experience post-operative pain and discomfort. Patients are encouraged to contact staff for pain
medication before the pain becomes intolerable or if the pain medication is not working. Controlling your pain is a key factor to
effectively participate in your therapy.
Description:
Description:
Give patient/caregiver written information about safety devices being used, if applicable. Explain reason for use.
Description:
Explain to patient/family about other ways of controlling pain than medications. The following are suggestions: position change,
aromatherapy, deep slow breathing exercises, decrease environmental stimuli,distraction, quiet environment, guided imagery,
Patient Friendly Description: For patient safety, you may see different pieces of equipment in your room. This could include gait belts,
walkers, wheelchairs and other pieces of equipment if applicable.
Provide patient with written information on the Pain Rating Scale, Explain the rating scale of 0 to 10 (both verbal and non-verbal) and its
purpose.
Encourage the patient to contact staff for pain medication before the pain becomes intolerable or if pain medication is not working.
Description:
Patient Friendly Description: Medication can be very helpful, but some pain medicines are more effective in controlling pain when they
are combined with other methods of treatment. Here are a few things we recommend. If you're able, do not stay in bed all day. Spend
some of the day in the chair and walk around your room and in the hall. Be sure to ask for help if you are not steady on your feet. Try
using ice packs whcih are available upon request if apropriate for your care. Deep breathing and visualizing your pain leaving your
body can be helpful. Mindfulness and meditation also addresses other emotions such as anxiety and fear. Splint with a pillow when
you cough, deep breathe, or when repositioning after surgery or a procedure.
Description:
Explain why there is a higher risk of falls in the hospital. Review ways to reduce the chance of falling. Explain safe room set up.
Provide medication specific handouts when appropriate.
Description:
Patient Friendly Description: I was provided with written information on the Pain Rating Scale. This information is included in "My
Patient Guide"as well as my Joint Replacement Guidebook.

Point: Discharge Planning (Not Started)
Patient Friendly Description: Call your doctor if you have a fever greater than 100.8 °F; signs of infection; unrelieved pain; symptoms
that are not relieved or worsening; chest pain or shortness of breath; or any change in your condition.
Description:
Description:
Help patient identify additional resources. Refer to Social Services/Pastoral Care/Case Management, if necessary.
Description:
Review the discharge plan with patient/family including follow-up appointments, diet, activity, medications and special precautions.
Explain that the patient's armband, and two identifiers will be checked before medications are given to the patient.
Patient Friendly Description: Your nurse can help you get in contact with your Case Manager or Social Worker if you have questions
about additional resources that may be necessary. Your Joint Replacement Guidebook also has a list of Community Resources.
Description:
Instruct learners on name and purpose of medications and possible side effects. If appropriate, include food/drug interactions, reporting
of efficacy, and symptoms to report.
Patient Friendly Description: The Social Worker or Case Manager will discuss with you any discharge needs that you may have. If
you are going home, someone will need to drive you there. However, if you are going to rehab or a skilled nursing facility,
transportation will be arranged for you. Therapists and nurses will review with you throughout your stay any restrictions that you may
have. You can review your activity restrictions and any special precautions in your Joint Replacement Guidebook.
Patient Friendly Description: I was provided with written information on Fall Prevention. This information is included in "My Patient
Guide".
Description:
Instruct patient/family to call the doctor if there is an increased temperature (greater than 100.8), unrelieved pain, symptoms not
relieved or worsening, chest pain, shortness of breath, side effects of medications or any change in condition.
Patient Friendly Description: When administering medications the nurse will scan your armband and check two identifiers before
medications are given to you for your safety.

Point: Post Operative Pain (Not Started)
Laproscopic: Explain to the patient/family that during the procedure, carbon dioxide gas was introduced into your abdomen to allow for
visualization of your organs. A very small amount of gas will remain in your body and is naturally absorbed and/or
expelled. You may notice your abdomen is bloated due to the remaining gas. Pain in your shoulders, neck, or abdomen may be
experienced for the next couple of days. The best way to expel this excess gas is ambulation.
Open: Explain to the patient/family that there will be pain at the incision site. Instruct patient regarding splinting incision with pillow for
turning, coughing, and deep breathing, and getting out of bed. Explain to patient pain in your abdomen may
be experienced due to the movement of gas. The best way to expel this gas is ambulation.
Learner
Progress:
Not documented in this visit.
Point: Resuming Intake (Not Started)
Point: Prevention of Post Operative Complications (Not Started)
activity and ambulation, including turning frequently while in bed, sitting up at meal times, and ambulating in hall. These interventions can
decrease likelihood of skin breakdown, increase comfort, and promote return of bowel function.
Patient Friendly Description: "Caregivers may give you medicine to take away or decrease your pain. Do not wait until the pain is
severe to ask for your medicine. Tell caregivers if your pain does not decrease. The medicine may not work as well at controlling your
pain if you wait too long to take it. Pain medicine can make you dizzy or sleepy. Prevent falls by calling a caregiver when you want to
get out of bed or if you need help.
If you had a procedure/surgery that was done laparoscopically, carbon dioxide gas was introduced into your abdomen to allow for
visualization of your organs. A very small amount of gas will remain in your body and is naturally absorbed and/or
expelled. You may notice your abdomen is bloated due to the remaining gas and the best way to expel this excess gas is by increased
activity, such as walking with assistance in the halls. Pain in your shoulders, neck, or abdomen may be experienced for the next
couple of days.
If you had a procedure/surgery that required an open incision, there will be pain at the incision site. Splinting the incision with a pillow
when turning, coughing, and deep breathing, and getting out of bed can help alleviate the pain. Pain in your abdomen may
be experienced due to the movement of gas and the best way to expel this gas is by increased activity, such as walking with
assistance in the halls."
Description:
Provide information regarding post op pain control. Discuss the pain scale (1-10) and currently ordered pain medication.
Description:
Patient Friendly Description: Your nurse will inform you on how and when to resume/progress your activity following the
procedure/surgery, including any specific limitations or restrictions that should be followed.
Description:
Explain purpose/use of incentive spirometer. Indicate reason for using incentive is to exercise the lungs to prevent pneumonia and
other respiratory complications. Explain purpose/use of teds and scds is to decrease risk of DVT. Encourage progressive
Patient Friendly Description: Your nurse will inform you on how and when to resume/progress your diet following the
procedure/surgery.
Description:
Give explanation on when to resume/progress activity.
Give explanation on how and when to resume/progress diet.

Point: Post Operative Drain/Incision Care (Not Started)
Patient Friendly Description: "Your nurse will educate you on the care of any incision(s) and the surrounding skin, including ways to
protect the incision by keeping it clean and dry and performing dressing changes. Some common signs and symptoms of infection to
monitor for, include: pain, redness or swelling at the incision; a change in the amount, color or odor of drainage.
If you have any post-operative drains, your nurse will inform you of their purpose and educate you on specific care instructions and
how to recognize changes in amount and type of drainage from the drain. "
Patient Friendly Description: You may be given an incentive spirometer, which is used to exercise the lungs to prevent pneumonia and
other respiratory complications. You may also have supplies/equipment such as TED hose or SCDs used to decrease the risk of a
Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT), or a blood clot that forms in a deep vein of the body. TED hose are stockings that help prevent blood
clots and swelling in your legs. Sequential Compression Devices (SCDs) are inflatable sleeves used to improve blood flow. You may
be encouraged to perform interventions that can decrease the likelihood of skin breakdown, increase comfort and promote the return
of bowel function, including: turning frequently while in bed, sitting up at meal times, and ambulating in the halls.
Description:
Explain purpose of drain and instruct on care. Teach patient how to recognize changes in amount and type of drainage from drain.
Explain care of incision as well as peri-wound skin, including not touching the incision, keeping the incision clean and

Point: Pain Rating Scale (Not Started)
Patient Friendly Description: I was provided with written information on the Pain Rating Scale. This information is included in "My
Patient Guide"as well as my Joint Replacement Guidebook.
Patient Friendly Description: You will experience post-operative pain and discomfort. Patients are encouraged to contact staff for pain
medication before the pain becomes intolerable or if the pain medication is not working. Controlling your pain is a key factor to
effectively participate in your therapy.
Description:
Description:
Give patient/caregiver written information about safety devices being used, if applicable. Explain reason for use.
Description:
Explain to patient/family about other ways of controlling pain than medications. The following are suggestions: position change,
aromatherapy, deep slow breathing exercises, decrease environmental stimuli,distraction, quiet environment, guided imagery,
Patient Friendly Description: For patient safety, you may see different pieces of equipment in your room. This could include gait belts,
walkers, wheelchairs and other pieces of equipment if applicable.
Provide patient with written information on the Pain Rating Scale, Explain the rating scale of 0 to 10 (both verbal and non-verbal) and its
purpose.
Encourage the patient to contact staff for pain medication before the pain becomes intolerable or if pain medication is not working.
Description:
Patient Friendly Description: Medication can be very helpful, but some pain medicines are more effective in controlling pain when they
are combined with other methods of treatment. Here are a few things we recommend. If you're able, do not stay in bed all day. Spend
some of the day in the chair and walk around your room and in the hall. Be sure to ask for help if you are not steady on your feet. Try
using ice packs whcih are available upon request if apropriate for your care. Deep breathing and visualizing your pain leaving your
body can be helpful. Mindfulness and meditation also addresses other emotions such as anxiety and fear. Splint with a pillow when
you cough, deep breathe, or when repositioning after surgery or a procedure.
Provide medication specific handouts when appropriate.
Description:

Point: Discharge Planning (Not Started)
Instruct patient/family to call the doctor if there is an increased temperature (greater than 100.8), unrelieved pain, symptoms not
relieved or worsening, chest pain, shortness of breath, side effects of medications or any change in condition.
Description:
Explain that the patient's armband, and two identifiers will be checked before medications are given to the patient.
Patient Friendly Description: Call your doctor if you have a fever greater than 100.8 °F; signs of infection; unrelieved pain; symptoms
that are not relieved or worsening; chest pain or shortness of breath; or any change in your condition.
Explain why there is a higher risk of falls in the hospital. Review ways to reduce the chance of falling. Explain safe room set up.
Description:
Patient Friendly Description: I was provided with written information on Fall Prevention. This information is included in "My Patient
Guide".
Description:
Help patient identify additional resources. Refer to Social Services/Pastoral Care/Case Management, if necessary.
Patient Friendly Description: When administering medications the nurse will scan your armband and check two identifiers before
medications are given to you for your safety.
Description:
Patient Friendly Description: Your nurse can help you get in contact with your Case Manager or Social Worker if you have questions
about additional resources that may be necessary. Your Joint Replacement Guidebook also has a list of Community Resources.
Review the discharge plan with patient/family including follow-up appointments, diet, activity, medications and special precautions.
Description:
Instruct learners on name and purpose of medications and possible side effects. If appropriate, include food/drug interactions, reporting
of efficacy, and symptoms to report.
Patient Friendly Description: The Social Worker or Case Manager will discuss with you any discharge needs that you may have. If
you are going home, someone will need to drive you there. However, if you are going to rehab or a skilled nursing facility,
transportation will be arranged for you. Therapists and nurses will review with you throughout your stay any restrictions that you may
have. You can review your activity restrictions and any special precautions in your Joint Replacement Guidebook.
Description:

Point: Post Operative Pain (Not Started)
Laproscopic: Explain to the patient/family that during the procedure, carbon dioxide gas was introduced into your abdomen to allow for
visualization of your organs. A very small amount of gas will remain in your body and is naturally absorbed and/or
expelled. You may notice your abdomen is bloated due to the remaining gas. Pain in your shoulders, neck, or abdomen may be
experienced for the next couple of days. The best way to expel this excess gas is ambulation.
Open: Explain to the patient/family that there will be pain at the incision site. Instruct patient regarding splinting incision with pillow for
turning, coughing, and deep breathing, and getting out of bed. Explain to patient pain in your abdomen may
be experienced due to the movement of gas. The best way to expel this gas is ambulation.
Learner
Progress:
Not documented in this visit.
Point: Prevention of Post Operative Complications (Not Started)
Learner
Progress:
Not documented in this visit.
Point: Resuming Intake (Not Started)
Patient Friendly Description: Your nurse will inform you on how and when to resume/progress your activity following the
procedure/surgery, including any specific limitations or restrictions that should be followed.
Description:
Give explanation on how and when to resume/progress diet.
Instruct learners on name and purpose of medications and possible side effects. If appropriate, include food/drug interactions, reporting
of efficacy, and symptoms to report.
Description:
Patient Friendly Description: "Caregivers may give you medicine to take away or decrease your pain. Do not wait until the pain is
severe to ask for your medicine. Tell caregivers if your pain does not decrease. The medicine may not work as well at controlling your
pain if you wait too long to take it. Pain medicine can make you dizzy or sleepy. Prevent falls by calling a caregiver when you want to
get out of bed or if you need help.
If you had a procedure/surgery that was done laparoscopically, carbon dioxide gas was introduced into your abdomen to allow for
visualization of your organs. A very small amount of gas will remain in your body and is naturally absorbed and/or
expelled. You may notice your abdomen is bloated due to the remaining gas and the best way to expel this excess gas is by increased
activity, such as walking with assistance in the halls. Pain in your shoulders, neck, or abdomen may be experienced for the next
couple of days.
If you had a procedure/surgery that required an open incision, there will be pain at the incision site. Splinting the incision with a pillow
when turning, coughing, and deep breathing, and getting out of bed can help alleviate the pain. Pain in your abdomen may
be experienced due to the movement of gas and the best way to expel this gas is by increased activity, such as walking with
assistance in the halls."
Description:
Description:
Provide information regarding post op pain control. Discuss the pain scale (1-10) and currently ordered pain medication.
Give explanation on when to resume/progress activity.
Patient Friendly Description: Your nurse will inform you on how and when to resume/progress your diet following the
procedure/surgery.

activity and ambulation, including turning frequently while in bed, sitting up at meal times, and ambulating in hall. These interventions can

If you have any post-operative drains, your nurse will inform you of their purpose and educate you on specific care instructions and

Description:
Description:
Explain purpose of drain and instruct on care. Teach patient how to recognize changes in amount and type of drainage from drain.
Explain care of incision as well as peri-wound skin, including not touching the incision, keeping the incision clean and
Explain purpose/use of incentive spirometer. Indicate reason for using incentive is to exercise the lungs to prevent pneumonia and
other respiratory complications. Explain purpose/use of teds and scds is to decrease risk of DVT. Encourage progressive
Patient Friendly Description: You may be given an incentive spirometer, which is used to exercise the lungs to prevent pneumonia and
other respiratory complications. You may also have supplies/equipment such as TED hose or SCDs used to decrease the risk of a
Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT), or a blood clot that forms in a deep vein of the body. TED hose are stockings that help prevent blood
clots and swelling in your legs. Sequential Compression Devices (SCDs) are inflatable sleeves used to improve blood flow. You may
be encouraged to perform interventions that can decrease the likelihood of skin breakdown, increase comfort and promote the return
of bowel function, including: turning frequently while in bed, sitting up at meal times, and ambulating in the halls.
Patient Friendly Description: "Your nurse will educate you on the care of any incision(s) and the surrounding skin, including ways to
protect the incision by keeping it clean and dry and performing dressing changes. Some common signs and symptoms of infection to
monitor for, include: pain, redness or swelling at the incision; a change in the amount, color or odor of drainage.

Point: Pain Rating Scale (Not Started)
Point: Fall Prevention (Not Started)
Patient Friendly Description: You will experience post-operative pain and discomfort. Patients are encouraged to contact staff for pain
medication before the pain becomes intolerable or if the pain medication is not working. Controlling your pain is a key factor to
effectively participate in your therapy.
Description:
Description:
Give patient/caregiver written information about safety devices being used, if applicable. Explain reason for use.
Description:
Explain to patient/family about other ways of controlling pain than medications. The following are suggestions: position change,
aromatherapy, deep slow breathing exercises, decrease environmental stimuli,distraction, quiet environment, guided imagery,
Patient Friendly Description: For patient safety, you may see different pieces of equipment in your room. This could include gait belts,
walkers, wheelchairs and other pieces of equipment if applicable.
Provide patient with written information on the Pain Rating Scale, Explain the rating scale of 0 to 10 (both verbal and non-verbal) and its
purpose.
Encourage the patient to contact staff for pain medication before the pain becomes intolerable or if pain medication is not working.
Description:
Patient Friendly Description: Medication can be very helpful, but some pain medicines are more effective in controlling pain when they
are combined with other methods of treatment. Here are a few things we recommend. If you're able, do not stay in bed all day. Spend
some of the day in the chair and walk around your room and in the hall. Be sure to ask for help if you are not steady on your feet. Try
using ice packs whcih are available upon request if apropriate for your care. Deep breathing and visualizing your pain leaving your
body can be helpful. Mindfulness and meditation also addresses other emotions such as anxiety and fear. Splint with a pillow when
you cough, deep breathe, or when repositioning after surgery or a procedure.
Description:
Explain why there is a higher risk of falls in the hospital. Review ways to reduce the chance of falling. Explain safe room set up.
Provide medication specific handouts when appropriate.
Description:
Patient Friendly Description: I was provided with written information on the Pain Rating Scale. This information is included in "My
Patient Guide"as well as my Joint Replacement Guidebook.

Patient Friendly Description: Call your doctor if you have a fever greater than 100.8 °F; signs of infection; unrelieved pain; symptoms
that are not relieved or worsening; chest pain or shortness of breath; or any change in your condition.
Description:
Description:
Help patient identify additional resources. Refer to Social Services/Pastoral Care/Case Management, if necessary.
Description:
Review the discharge plan with patient/family including follow-up appointments, diet, activity, medications and special precautions.
Explain that the patient's armband, and two identifiers will be checked before medications are given to the patient.
Patient Friendly Description: Your nurse can help you get in contact with your Case Manager or Social Worker if you have questions
about additional resources that may be necessary. Your Joint Replacement Guidebook also has a list of Community Resources.
Description:
Instruct learners on name and purpose of medications and possible side effects. If appropriate, include food/drug interactions, reporting
of efficacy, and symptoms to report.
Patient Friendly Description: The Social Worker or Case Manager will discuss with you any discharge needs that you may have. If
you are going home, someone will need to drive you there. However, if you are going to rehab or a skilled nursing facility,
transportation will be arranged for you. Therapists and nurses will review with you throughout your stay any restrictions that you may
have. You can review your activity restrictions and any special precautions in your Joint Replacement Guidebook.
Patient Friendly Description: I was provided with written information on Fall Prevention. This information is included in "My Patient
Guide".
Description:
Instruct patient/family to call the doctor if there is an increased temperature (greater than 100.8), unrelieved pain, symptoms not
relieved or worsening, chest pain, shortness of breath, side effects of medications or any change in condition.
Patient Friendly Description: When administering medications the nurse will scan your armband and check two identifiers before
medications are given to you for your safety.

Point: Post Operative Pain (Not Started)
Laproscopic: Explain to the patient/family that during the procedure, carbon dioxide gas was introduced into your abdomen to allow for
visualization of your organs. A very small amount of gas will remain in your body and is naturally absorbed and/or
expelled. You may notice your abdomen is bloated due to the remaining gas. Pain in your shoulders, neck, or abdomen may be
experienced for the next couple of days. The best way to expel this excess gas is ambulation.
Open: Explain to the patient/family that there will be pain at the incision site. Instruct patient regarding splinting incision with pillow for
turning, coughing, and deep breathing, and getting out of bed. Explain to patient pain in your abdomen may
be experienced due to the movement of gas. The best way to expel this gas is ambulation.
Learner
Progress:
Not documented in this visit.
Point: Resuming Intake (Not Started)
Point: Prevention of Post Operative Complications (Not Started)
activity and ambulation, including turning frequently while in bed, sitting up at meal times, and ambulating in hall. These interventions can
decrease likelihood of skin breakdown, increase comfort, and promote return of bowel function.
Patient Friendly Description: "Caregivers may give you medicine to take away or decrease your pain. Do not wait until the pain is
severe to ask for your medicine. Tell caregivers if your pain does not decrease. The medicine may not work as well at controlling your
pain if you wait too long to take it. Pain medicine can make you dizzy or sleepy. Prevent falls by calling a caregiver when you want to
get out of bed or if you need help.
If you had a procedure/surgery that was done laparoscopically, carbon dioxide gas was introduced into your abdomen to allow for
visualization of your organs. A very small amount of gas will remain in your body and is naturally absorbed and/or
expelled. You may notice your abdomen is bloated due to the remaining gas and the best way to expel this excess gas is by increased
activity, such as walking with assistance in the halls. Pain in your shoulders, neck, or abdomen may be experienced for the next
couple of days.
If you had a procedure/surgery that required an open incision, there will be pain at the incision site. Splinting the incision with a pillow
when turning, coughing, and deep breathing, and getting out of bed can help alleviate the pain. Pain in your abdomen may
be experienced due to the movement of gas and the best way to expel this gas is by increased activity, such as walking with
assistance in the halls."
Description:
Provide information regarding post op pain control. Discuss the pain scale (1-10) and currently ordered pain medication.
Description:
Patient Friendly Description: Your nurse will inform you on how and when to resume/progress your activity following the
procedure/surgery, including any specific limitations or restrictions that should be followed.
Description:
Explain purpose/use of incentive spirometer. Indicate reason for using incentive is to exercise the lungs to prevent pneumonia and
other respiratory complications. Explain purpose/use of teds and scds is to decrease risk of DVT. Encourage progressive
Patient Friendly Description: Your nurse will inform you on how and when to resume/progress your diet following the
procedure/surgery.
Description:
Give explanation on when to resume/progress activity.
Give explanation on how and when to resume/progress diet.

Description:
Explain purpose of drain and instruct on care. Teach patient how to recognize changes in amount and type of drainage from drain.
Explain care of incision as well as peri-wound skin, including not touching the incision, keeping the incision clean and
Patient Friendly Description: "Your nurse will educate you on the care of any incision(s) and the surrounding skin, including ways to
protect the incision by keeping it clean and dry and performing dressing changes. Some common signs and symptoms of infection to
monitor for, include: pain, redness or swelling at the incision; a change in the amount, color or odor of drainage.
If you have any post-operative drains, your nurse will inform you of their purpose and educate you on specific care instructions and
how to recognize changes in amount and type of drainage from the drain. "
Patient Friendly Description: You may be given an incentive spirometer, which is used to exercise the lungs to prevent pneumonia and
other respiratory complications. You may also have supplies/equipment such as TED hose or SCDs used to decrease the risk of a
Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT), or a blood clot that forms in a deep vein of the body. TED hose are stockings that help prevent blood
clots and swelling in your legs. Sequential Compression Devices (SCDs) are inflatable sleeves used to improve blood flow. You may
be encouraged to perform interventions that can decrease the likelihood of skin breakdown, increase comfort and promote the return
of bowel function, including: turning frequently while in bed, sitting up at meal times, and ambulating in the halls.

Point: Pain Rating Scale (Not Started)
Patient Friendly Description: I was provided with written information on the Pain Rating Scale. This information is included in "My
Patient Guide"as well as my Joint Replacement Guidebook.
Patient Friendly Description: You will experience post-operative pain and discomfort. Patients are encouraged to contact staff for pain
medication before the pain becomes intolerable or if the pain medication is not working. Controlling your pain is a key factor to
effectively participate in your therapy.
Description:
Description:
To protect self and others from infection, patient/family should cover nose and mouth with tissues when coughing. Patient/family should
always wash their hands after using or handling used tissues.
Description:
Explain to patient/family about other ways of controlling pain than medications. The following are suggestions: position change,
aromatherapy, deep slow breathing exercises, decrease environmental stimuli,distraction, quiet environment, guided imagery,
Patient Friendly Description: You can protect yourself and others from infection by covering your nose and mouth with tissues when
coughing and washing hands after using or handling used tissues.
Provide patient with written information on the Pain Rating Scale, Explain the rating scale of 0 to 10 (both verbal and non-verbal) and its
purpose.
Encourage the patient to contact staff for pain medication before the pain becomes intolerable or if pain medication is not working.
Description:
Patient Friendly Description: Medication can be very helpful, but some pain medicines are more effective in controlling pain when they
are combined with other methods of treatment. Here are a few things we recommend. If you're able, do not stay in bed all day. Spend
some of the day in the chair and walk around your room and in the hall. Be sure to ask for help if you are not steady on your feet. Try
using ice packs whcih are available upon request if apropriate for your care. Deep breathing and visualizing your pain leaving your
body can be helpful. Mindfulness and meditation also addresses other emotions such as anxiety and fear. Splint with a pillow when
you cough, deep breathe, or when repositioning after surgery or a procedure.
Provide medication specific handouts when appropriate.
Description:

Description:
Description:
Encourage the patient to turn and move in bed as much as possible. Explain the reason for turning, coughing and deep breathing.
Instruct the patient to take 10 deep breaths in a row every hour while awake. Remind the patient to follow each deep breathe
Demonstrate proper hand hygiene. Handwashing is the single most important step in preventing spread of germs. Use soap, water, and
friction for at least 15-30 seconds. Clean nails under running water. Dry hands on clean towel without touching dirty
Description:
Give patient/caregiver written information about safety devices being used, if applicable. Explain reason for use.
Patient Friendly Description: I was provided with written information on handwashing. This information is included in "My Patient
Guide".
Description:
Patient Friendly Description: For patient safety, you may see different pieces of equipment in your room. This could include gait belts,
walkers, wheelchairs and other pieces of equipment if applicable.
Review the signs and symptoms of infections; fever, redness at insertion site, unusual drainage at insertion site, and warmth at
insertion site.
Description:
Provide instruction on the importance of consuming adequate nutrition/hydration to support the healing process. Provide information on
patient's diet. Refer to Clinical Dietitian, when appropriate.
Description:
Explain why there is a higher risk of falls in the hospital. Review ways to reduce the chance of falling. Explain safe room set up.
Patient Friendly Description: It is important to consume adequate nutrition and hydration to support the healing process. I was
educated on the diet my physician has ordered.
Patient Friendly Description: Signs and symptoms of infection include increased redness or swelling at incision site, persistent
redness, hardness or heat, change in color, amount or odor of drainage, increased pain, or fever greater than 100.5 by mouth.
Patient Friendly Description: Patients are encouraged to turn and move in bed as much as possible and also turn, cough and deep
breathe. The reason for this is to lower your risk for lung complications, such as pneumonia. Patients should take 10 deep breaths in
a row every hour while awake and follow each deep breath with a cough.

Point: Medication Safety (Not Started)
Instruct and teach, repositioning q2h, elevating pressure points off the bed, avoiding pressure over bony areas, maximizing patient's
mobility, ensuring adequate nutrition/hydration intake, keep skin clean and dry, applying heel & elbow protectors and
Description:
Help patient identify additional resources. Refer to Social Services/Pastoral Care/Case Management, if necessary.
Patient Friendly Description: Your nurse can help you get in contact with your Case Manager or Social Worker if you have questions
about additional resources that may be necessary. Your Joint Replacement Guidebook also has a list of Community Resources.
Description:
Review the discharge plan with patient/family including follow-up appointments, diet, activity, medications and special precautions.
Patient Friendly Description: When administering medications the nurse will scan your armband and check two identifiers before
medications are given to you for your safety.
Patient Friendly Description: To prevent skin breakdown patients should reposition every 2 hours, work with the nurse to elevate
pressure points off the bed, maximize mobility, ensure adequate nutrition and hydration, keep skin clean and dry and wear heel or
elbow protectors and protective barriers when appropriate.
Description:
Instruct patient/family to call the doctor if there is an increased temperature (greater than 100.8), unrelieved pain, symptoms not
relieved or worsening, chest pain, shortness of breath, side effects of medications or any change in condition.
Patient Friendly Description: The Social Worker or Case Manager will discuss with you any discharge needs that you may have. If
you are going home, someone will need to drive you there. However, if you are going to rehab or a skilled nursing facility,
transportation will be arranged for you. Therapists and nurses will review with you throughout your stay any restrictions that you may
have. You can review your activity restrictions and any special precautions in your Joint Replacement Guidebook.
Patient Friendly Description: Call your doctor if you have a fever greater than 100.8 °F; signs of infection; unrelieved pain; symptoms
that are not relieved or worsening; chest pain or shortness of breath; or any change in your condition.
Description:
Explain that the patient's armband, and two identifiers will be checked before medications are given to the patient.
Patient Friendly Description: I was provided with written information on Fall Prevention. This information is included in "My Patient
Guide".

Point: Post Operative Pain (Not Started)
Laproscopic: Explain to the patient/family that during the procedure, carbon dioxide gas was introduced into your abdomen to allow for
visualization of your organs. A very small amount of gas will remain in your body and is naturally absorbed and/or
expelled. You may notice your abdomen is bloated due to the remaining gas. Pain in your shoulders, neck, or abdomen may be
experienced for the next couple of days. The best way to expel this excess gas is ambulation.
Open: Explain to the patient/family that there will be pain at the incision site. Instruct patient regarding splinting incision with pillow for
turning, coughing, and deep breathing, and getting out of bed. Explain to patient pain in your abdomen may
be experienced due to the movement of gas. The best way to expel this gas is ambulation.

Point: Resuming Intake (Not Started)
Patient Friendly Description: Your nurse will inform you on how and when to resume/progress your activity following the
procedure/surgery, including any specific limitations or restrictions that should be followed.
Description:
Give explanation on how and when to resume/progress diet.
Instruct learners on name and purpose of medications and possible side effects. If appropriate, include food/drug interactions, reporting
of efficacy, and symptoms to report.
Description:
Patient Friendly Description: "Caregivers may give you medicine to take away or decrease your pain. Do not wait until the pain is
severe to ask for your medicine. Tell caregivers if your pain does not decrease. The medicine may not work as well at controlling your
pain if you wait too long to take it. Pain medicine can make you dizzy or sleepy. Prevent falls by calling a caregiver when you want to
get out of bed or if you need help.
If you had a procedure/surgery that was done laparoscopically, carbon dioxide gas was introduced into your abdomen to allow for
visualization of your organs. A very small amount of gas will remain in your body and is naturally absorbed and/or
expelled. You may notice your abdomen is bloated due to the remaining gas and the best way to expel this excess gas is by increased
activity, such as walking with assistance in the halls. Pain in your shoulders, neck, or abdomen may be experienced for the next
couple of days.
If you had a procedure/surgery that required an open incision, there will be pain at the incision site. Splinting the incision with a pillow
when turning, coughing, and deep breathing, and getting out of bed can help alleviate the pain. Pain in your abdomen may
be experienced due to the movement of gas and the best way to expel this gas is by increased activity, such as walking with
assistance in the halls."
Description:
Description:
Provide information regarding post op pain control. Discuss the pain scale (1-10) and currently ordered pain medication.
Give explanation on when to resume/progress activity.
Patient Friendly Description: Your nurse will inform you on how and when to resume/progress your diet following the
procedure/surgery.

dry. Monitor for signs and symptoms of infection including redness, firmness, change in amount, color, or odor of drainage, open areas of

Progress:
Not documented in this visit.
activity and ambulation, including turning frequently while in bed, sitting up at meal times, and ambulating in hall. These interventions can
decrease likelihood of skin breakdown, increase comfort, and promote return of bowel function.
If you have any post-operative drains, your nurse will inform you of their purpose and educate you on specific care instructions and
how to recognize changes in amount and type of drainage from the drain. "
Description:
Description:
Explain purpose of drain and instruct on care. Teach patient how to recognize changes in amount and type of drainage from drain.
Explain care of incision as well as peri-wound skin, including not touching the incision, keeping the incision clean and
Explain purpose/use of incentive spirometer. Indicate reason for using incentive is to exercise the lungs to prevent pneumonia and
other respiratory complications. Explain purpose/use of teds and scds is to decrease risk of DVT. Encourage progressive
Patient Friendly Description: You may be given an incentive spirometer, which is used to exercise the lungs to prevent pneumonia and
other respiratory complications. You may also have supplies/equipment such as TED hose or SCDs used to decrease the risk of a
Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT), or a blood clot that forms in a deep vein of the body. TED hose are stockings that help prevent blood
clots and swelling in your legs. Sequential Compression Devices (SCDs) are inflatable sleeves used to improve blood flow. You may
be encouraged to perform interventions that can decrease the likelihood of skin breakdown, increase comfort and promote the return
of bowel function, including: turning frequently while in bed, sitting up at meal times, and ambulating in the halls.
Patient Friendly Description: "Your nurse will educate you on the care of any incision(s) and the surrounding skin, including ways to
protect the incision by keeping it clean and dry and performing dressing changes. Some common signs and symptoms of infection to
monitor for, include: pain, redness or swelling at the incision; a change in the amount, color or odor of drainage.

Procedure: XR HIP LEFT W-OR-WO PELVIS 2-3 VIEWS
Result Date: 8/24/2023
8/21/2023 L hip xray shows stable position of L THRA without evidence of loosening or wear of prosthesis.
Procedure: US TRANSVAGINAL NON-OB
Result Date: 7/21/2023
INDICATION: Pelvic organ prolapse. TECHNIQUE: High-resolution endovaginal gray scale images were
performed of the uterus, ovaries, endometrium and adnexa. Color Doppler of the ovaries was performed.
COMPARISON: None FINDINGS: Uterus is normal in size and homogeneous in echotexture without a focal
abnormality. Uterine length is 7.7cm and the volume is 77 cc. Endometrium is normal in thickness measuring
0.8 cm. Right ovary is normal in size and echotexture and shows blood flow on Doppler. Ovarian volume is 5.5
mL. Left ovary is enlarged with a volume of 42 mL secondary to two cysts with one of them a simple cyst
measuring 3.7 cm. Second cyst is also simple, but shows a septation through it measuring 1.9 cm.. No free
fluid is seen in the cul-de-sac.
Procedure: US VENOUS DOPPLER LOWER EXTREMITY BILATERAL
Result Date: 7/21/2023
HISTORY: Leg swelling. TECHNIQUE: High-resolution gray scale imaging, color flow Doppler, compression
were performed from the groin to the calf of the bilateral lower extremities Augmentation was performed of the
bilateral common femoral vein and popliteal vein. FINDINGS: Normal color flow and compressibility,
augmentation, phasicity and competency of the common femoral, femoral and popliteal veins. There is normal
compressibility of the calf trifurcation. No evidence of deep vein thrombosis.
Procedure: XR HIP LEFT W-OR-WO PELVIS 2-3 VIEWS
Result Date: 7/14/2023
Imaging reviewed: 07/13/23: views of the left hip show stable position of total hip arthroplasty. No evidence of
loosening or wear

I reviewed and summarized pertinent portions of the old record as shown above. Pertinent labs and diagnostic
imaging were also reviewed and reproduced above.
Plan:
Kimberly K Brasher is a 50 y.o. female who presents to clinic for:
Chronic postoperative left hip pain
Anxiety
Depression
Chronic left hip pain since her hip pinning surgery and THA (~4 months apart) which she had to get done for a
fracture from standing height after tripping in the parking lot. proMental health flared following separation from
her husband. Has been on Prozac. H/o RYGB, hence will avoid NSAIDs. She is not interested in injections.
She would prefer not to try TENS unit. She is agreeable to try stretches.
Start Physical Therapy, referral placed
Repeat CBC, HFP, Cr
Start duloxetine as follows:
Week 1: 20 mg daily
Week 2: 40 mg daily
Week 3: 40 mg daily
Discussed side effects including activation of mania/hypomania, suicidal thinking and/or behavior,
bleeding risk, fragility fractures, hepatotoxicity, hypernatremia, acute angle closure glaucoma, sexual
dysfunction, withdrawal syndrome, weight loss, decreased appetite, constipation, nausea, vomiting,
xerostomia, drowsiness, fatigue, headache, etc
Discussed potential for serotonin syndrome with concomitant use with fluoxetine and to stop and call us
should she encounter symptoms of fever/hyperpyrexia, rigidity, tremor, seizures, palpitations, agitation,
abdominal pain, diarrhea, etc.
Start TENS treatment, see patient instructions/AVS
Avoid in the setting of pacemaker, pregnancy, or over any areas of cancer or open or healing
wounds

Start lower back exercises at home, see AVS
Start Heating pad PRN
Future options
o Acupuncture
Osteoporosis
Fragility fracture/closed left hip fracture after falling from standing height. H/o RYGB is a risk factor. Denies
tobacco or alcohol use. Does vape at times. Discussed plausible association between chronic PPI therapy and
increased risk for osteoporosis-related fractures. Has not been on SERMs, antiresorptive, or anabolic
medications before for her bones.
Encouraged regular weight bearing exercises
Emphasized alcohol and smoking cessation/avoidance as both can increase risk for osteoporosis
Discussed dietary recommendations including 1200-1500mg/day of calcium intake and 800-1200IU/day of
vitamin D intake.
25 OH Vit D level, iPTH, CMP (alk phos, albumin, calcium, Cr), phosphorus, magnesium, TSH ordered
Episodic migraine
Uses imitrex 5-6x per month. Counseled not to take abortive medications for her headaches more than 8-10
days per month to avoid medication over use headaches.
Start trial of OTC Magnesium (e.g. tricomplex formulation: Mg L-threonate, Mg Glycinate, and Mg Taurate)
Future options
o Acupuncture
Assessment and plan discussed with patient. Patient voiced understanding, agreeable, and would like to
proceed. Instructed to call the clinic for questions or concerns prior to next appointment.
Coordination of care
Ms. Brasher is recommended to follow up with her PCP, Vansickle, Logan S and other involved health care
providers to coordinate the above care plan
Next follow up: 8 weeks
History of Present Illness:
Kimberly K Brasher is a 50 y.o. female who presents to clinic for consultation.
Interval History:
12/1/2023
- S/p left hip pinning s/p replacement
- Since has had chronic right hip pain
- Has been more conscious of finding her step but no recurrent falls
- Tried naproxen, ibuprofen
- Had RYGB in 2007 and lost 100 lbs
